const int MAX_N = 50;
int m;
bool compressed[MAX_N];
int degree[MAX_N];
int g[MAX_N][MAX_N];
int dp[MAX_N];

// finds the path with the maximum number of compressed nodes starting at
// node x in the SCC graph.  
int rec(int x) {
  int res = dp[x];
  if (res == -1) {
    res = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < degree[x]; i++) {
      res = std::max(res, rec(g[x][i]));
    }
    if (compressed[x]) {
      res++;
    }
  }
  dp[x] = res;
  return res;
}

int minK(vector < string > graph) {
  // Find the SCCs in the graph:
  int n = graph.size();
  int reach[n][n];
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
      reach[i][j] = ((graph[i][j] == 'Y') ? 1 : 0);
    }
  }
  for (int k = 0; k < n; k++) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
      for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
        reach[i][j] |= reach[i][k] & reach[k][j];
      }
    }
  }
  vector < int > sccID(n, -1);
  m = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    if (sccID[i] == -1) {
      vector < int > scc(1, i);
      sccID[i] = m;

      compressed[m] = false;
      //cout << "SCC: "<<i;
      for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
        if ((reach[i][j] == 1) && (reach[j][i] == 1)) {
          compressed[m] = true; //this SCC gets compressed
          //cout << ", "<<j;
          scc.push_back(j);
          sccID[j] = m;
        }
      }
      //cout << endl;

      if (scc.size() > 1) {
        // check if the scc is more than just a cycle:
        for (int j: scc) {
          int degree = 0;
          for (int k: scc) {
            if ((j != k) && reach[j][k] && (graph[j][k] == 'Y')) {
              degree++;
            }
          }
          if (degree != 1) {
            return -1;
          }
        }
      }
      m++;
    }
  }
  // now build the SCC graph
  for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
    set < int > con;
    for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
      for (int k = 0; k < n; k++) {
        if ((graph[j][k] == 'Y') && (sccID[j] == i) && (sccID[k] != i)) {
          con.insert(sccID[k]);
        }
      }
    }
    degree[i] = 0;
    for (int j: con) {
      g[i][degree[i]++] = j;
    }
  }
  // Find the path in the SCC graph that contains the most compressed nodes.
  memset(dp, -1, sizeof(dp));
  int mx = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
    mx = max(mx, rec(i));
  }
  return max(0, mx - 1);
}

OneDimensionalRobot

Problem Details

Used as: Division One - Level Three:

Value900
Submission Rate25 / 743 (3.36%)
Success Rate16 / 25 (64.00%)
High ScoreWJMZBMR for 676.01 points (17 mins 37 secs)
Average Score507.46 (for 16 correct submissions)

There can be up to 5000*5000 pairs of (a,b) to simulate and each simulation might need up to 5000 steps. So the problem is about how to avoid doing all these many simulations.

Since the problem asks for the sum of final positions rather than the specific positions, we might try to find a way to find the sums without finding the individual results. But for this specific problem that might not be such an useful approach.

Another thing we can try to do is to somehow calculate results for some (a,b) using the results for other pairs (a’,b’) as a base and thus saving computations. This will be the approach we will use. It is not easy to find exactly which other pair (a’,b’) to use. During a match, you might have to try various possibilities / directions before you can reach the following property:

The left off-set

Try this: Assume the result for (a-1,b+1) is already known. Can we use it to calculate the result for (a,b) more efficiently? This effectively means we move the negative and positive limits one unit to the left.

We have no choice other than try various test cases in paper.

First of all, a simple case, if neither (a,b) or (a-1,b+1) have ignored moves, then the final positions will be the same in both cases. If moves are never ignored, then executing the sequence of moves in its entirety will always finish in the same position. So we would have that the result for (a,b) is equal to the result for (a-1,b+1), but first we need a quick way to know if neither of the pairs cause movements to be ignored. We can use a simulation to get the minimum and maximum positions during a simulation that never gets the moves ignored. If max(-a-1,-a) = -a is larger than the left-most point or if min(b,b+1) = b is smaller than the right-most point, then at least one move will be ignored.
What to do in the other cases? At least one move is ignored either for (a,b) or (a-1, b+1). It will be simple after trying some test cases manually:

The top row of images represent the simulations for A = a-1, B = b+1 and the bottom row the cases when A = a, B = b. (In all cases, a = 3, b = 1.). In a single image, the top line represents the starting position (0), and each of the consecutive lines show a new step, the blue line shows the movement of the robot. It appears that in each of these cases, the final position for (a,b) is equal to the final position for (a-1,b+1) minus 1. How can we prove it?

The proof is simple when we focus only on the first ignored move. There are some different cases.

Left move ignored in (a-1,b+1)

In case the very first ignored move is a left move that occurs in (a-1,b+1)

The first ignored “L” happens after the robot reaches A=-a+1 and tries to move left. In the revised version, A=-a, so the robot can move. After this move though, the robot has reached the left-most extreme of the limiting box. All the remaining moves will behave in the same way for both (a,b) and (a-1,b+1), but since the limitting box has moved one unit left, the final position will be one unit left.

Right move ignored in (a-1,b+1)

This is a interesting case, if a right move is the first ignored one; then we can also be sure that after this move, the robot will be guaranteed to be at B in both cases. Then once again, the remaining moves will behave the same way relative to A and B, and A and B moved one unit left and so will the final position.

Left move ignored in (a,b) and no move ignored in (a-1,b+1)

This is not possible, there will be more left space in (a,b) than in (a-1,b+1).

Right move ignored in (a,b) and no move ignored in (a-1,b+1)

A familiar situation:

Code

We need O(1) time to make the result for (a,b) using the result of (a-1,b+1). There are O(“maxB”) different distances b - a, for each of them, we need to do the O(n) simulation exactly once and then we just need an O(“maxB”) iteration to generate all the remaining results with that distance. The overall complexity will be: O(n * “maxB” + “maxB”^2).

int ans[5001][5001];
/* assume we already concatenated the two string[]s to generate s: */
long theSum(string s, int minA, int maxA, int minB, int maxB) {
  // Simulate to find the left/right extremes
  int L = 0, R = 0;
  int x = 0;
  for (char ch: s) {
    x += ((ch == 'L') ? -1 : 1);
    L = std::max(L, -x);
    R = std::max(R, x);
  }
  long sum = 0;

  for (int a = minA; a <= maxA; a++) {
    for (int b = minB; b <= maxB; b++) {
      if (a - 1 >= minA && b + 1 <= maxB) {
        //result of (a-1, b+1) is known:
        ans[a][b] = ans[a - 1][b + 1];
        if (L > a - 1 || R > b) { // A move gets ignored
          ans[a][b]--;
        }
      } else if (a >= L && b >= R) {
        ans[a][b] = x;
      } else {
        //result of (a-1, b+1) is unknown, simulate:
        int y = 0;
        for (char ch: s) {
          y += ((ch == 'L') ? -1 : 1);
          y = std::max(y, -a);
          y = std::min(y, b);
        }
        ans[a][b] = y;
        // for each value of a != minA, there is at most one value
        // of b that gets simulated. For each value of b != maxB,
        // there is at most one value of b who gets simulated.
        // (minA, maxB), gets simulated.
        // In total we do O(maxB + maxA) simulations.
      }
      sum += ans[a][b];
    }
  }
  return sum;

}

K ‘>’ characters followed by K ‘<’ character. The allowed operations cannot modify the length of the string. So if the original string has length n, the final string will also have length n. Constraints indicate that n will be even. It follows that K = n/2, so the first K characters of the final string must be ‘>’ and the other characters ‘<’. This means we already know what the final string must be.

For each index i, we can find the character in that index for the final string: (If i < K = n/2 , then the character is ‘>’, it is ‘<’ otherwise). So we can compare this final character with the initial character, if they are different, the cost increments by one.

int minimalMoves(string S) {
  int n = S.size(), c = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    char ch; //the i-th character of the wanted final string
    if (i < n / 2) {
      ch = '>';
    } else {
      ch = '<';
    }
    if (S[i] != ch) {
      // cost increments
      c++;
    }
  }
  return c;
}

There are different ways to implement the logic. We can actually downright build the final string by creating a string of n/2 ‘>’ characters followed by n/2 ‘<’. Then the comparison is easier, like in the following python example:

class MagicalStringDiv2:
  def minimalMoves(self, S):
  n = len(S)
T = '>' * (n / 2) + '<' * (n / 2)
return sum(S[i] != T[i]
  for i in range(n))

PackingBallsDiv2

Problem Details

Used as: Division Two - Level Two:

Value500
Submission Rate1096 / 1236 (88.67%)
Success Rate378 / 1096 (34.49%)
High Scorethelosser for 494.67 points (2 mins 57 secs)
Average Score336.70 (for 378 correct submissions)

Solution summary

Fix the number of variety sets v (at most 100). Put as many balls as possible in these sets. The number of remaining sets for each color can be calculated as simple division.

Keep it simple

There are various approaches to this problem, you can try dynamic programming, or some ad hoc optimization based on there being only 3 kinds of colors. Let’s keep it simple, however, the following is an approach that has a relatively simple implementation.

There are two kinds of packages, the variety ones and the normal ones. The constraints are small, so we can try to make a decision for the number of variety or normal sets we will use in total. Let’s decide the number of variety sets v, and try to calculate the number of normal sets.

The good thing about fixing the number of variety sets is that we can maximize the number of balls of each type that will be used in those sets. Imagine you want to make one variety set and there are r red balls, g green balls and b blue balls: There is no reason not to make the variety set contain 3 balls. We can repeat this logic until there are only two kinds of balls or until all variety sets are decided. If there are only two kinds of balls, and we still have variety sets to fill, we can just put two balls in each remaining variety set and so and so.

This greedy approach to fill the v variety sets also allows us to notice that the maximum number of variety sets we might need is equal to the maximum number of balls of the same color. In that case all sets are variety sets. So we only need to try at most 100 values for v.

After we used this greedy logic, there will be max(0 , R - v) red balls, max(0 , G - v) green balls and max(0 , B - v) blue balls left. Every set we make using these balls must be a normal set, meaning that it must contain only balls of the same color. The maximum number of balls in a set is 3. For each ball color, if the remaining balls is t, we just need to distribute those t balls in the least possible number of sets containing at most 3 balls. This is the same as doing a division and possibly leaving one set for the remainder if the division is not even. So the number of normal sets for this color is: |t/3| (The ceiling function operation, round up).

int minPacks(int R, int G, int B) {
  vector < int > x = {
    R,
    G,
    B
  };
  int res = 100; //happens when we use 100 variety packages

  // Try each number v of variety packages:
  for (int v = 0; v <= 99; v++) {
    int p = v;
    for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
      // remaining balls of this color:
      int t = max(0, x[i] - v);
      // add ceil(t / 3):
      p += (t + 2) / 3;
    }
    res = std::min(res, p);
  }
  return res;
}

VocaloidsAndSongs

Problem Details

Value950

Success Rate162 / 207 (78.26%)
High Scoresn006 for 919.57 points (5 mins 12 secs)
Average Score572.25 (for 162 correct submissions)

Three singers must sing s songs possible in duet or trio. The number of songs each singer must sing is a, b and c, respectively. We can try to find an optimal substructure / a recursive solution.

Let’s say a function f(S,a,b,c) solves the problem. When S = 0, then there are no songs to sign, the number of songs each singer must sign must be 0: If (a = b = c = 0), there is exactly one valid way to do things: do nothing. Otherwise, it is impossible and there are 0 valid ways. This will be our base case.

Otherwise, there is at least one song. We should decide which of the singers will sign it and which won’t. There are at most 7 ways to do the assignment. (e.g. [Singer 1 sings / Singer 2 doesn’t sing / Singer 3 sings], three independent choices with 2 options each, yield 8 different combined choices, but at least one singer must sign, so the choice in which no singer signs is not valid). We can repeat the following logic for each of the possible combinations:

  • If singer 1 is chosen to sign, then the number of their required songs has to decrement: a’ = a - 1, else a’ = a.

  • If singer 2 is chosen to sign, b’ = b - 1, else b’ = b.

  • If singer 3 is chosen to sign, c’ = c - 1, else c’ = c.

  • S-1 songs remain and the new required songs for each singer are : (a’, b’, c’). We can calculate the number of ways to do the remaining assignments by calling f(S-1, a’, b’, c’).

  • f(S,a,b,c) will be equal to the sum of f(S-1, a’, b’, c’) for each valid (a’,b’,c’).

  • Note that a’, b’ and c’ might become negative. Those cases should be ignored because it is impossible to make a singer sign a negative number of songs.

This logic is good enough to solve the problem. Each f(S,a,b,c) will only need to call instances of the function with S-1 songs, so the recurrence relation is acyclic. There are O(S) different values of each of the parameters in the function, so there are O(S^4) different states. So we have a dynamic programming solution. If we use memoization to ensure each state is evaluated at most once , the total complexity will be O(S^4), because each state needs to sum 8 values.

const int MOD = 1000000007;
int mem[51][51][51][51];

VocaloidsAndSongs() {
  // did you know you can initialize arrays in the constructor?
  // fill mem table with -1:
  memset(mem, -1, sizeof(mem));
}

int count(int S, int a, int b, int c) {
  int res = mem[S][a][b][c];
  if (res == -1) {
    if (S == 0) {
      // Base case, all singers must have sung the required songs:
      res = ((a == 0 && b == 0 && c == 0) ? 1 : 0);
    } else {
      res = 0;
      // for each valid new (na,nb,nc):
      for (int na = a; na >= a - 1 && na >= 0; na--) {
        for (int nb = b; nb >= b - 1 && nb >= 0; nb--) {
          for (int nc = c; nc >= c - 1 && nc >= 0; nc--) {
            // at least one must sign a song:
            if (na != a || nb != b || nc != c) {
              res = (res + count(S - 1, na, nb, nc)) % MOD;
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
    mem[S][a][b][c] = res;
  }
  return res;
}

This is a problem with many approaches.

Fix K, is there a magic sub-sequence?

One of the things we could do is change the problem to “Can we make a magical string of length 2K?”. If we can answer this question then all we need to do is search for the maximum K we can use.

When K is fixed, we know the magical string will be K “>” characters followed by K “<” characters. We just need to check if it is possible to remove some characters from the original string to turn it into that magical string. This is the same as saying that the magical string must be a subsequence of the original string. Determining if a string is a subsequence of another is a classic problem that can be done in O(n) time. To check if T is a subsequence of S, find the smallest index i such that S[i] = T[0], Then the smallest index j > i such that S[j] = T[1] and so and so. This yields an O(n^2) algorithm, as we need to try K = n/2, n/2-1, …, 0.

int getLongest(string S) {
  int n = S.size();
  for (int k = n / 2; k > 0; k--) {
    int j = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n && j < 2 * k; i++) {
      // find the j-th character of the wanted magical string:
      char ch = ((j < k) ? '>' : '<');
      // compare
      if (S[i] == ch) {
        j++;
      }
    }
    if (j >= 2 * k) {
      return 2 * k;
    }
  }
  return 0;
}

Recursive approach

A recursive approach, we solve f(S) that returns the maximum length of a magical string obtained after removing characters from S: Let l be the minimum position of “>” in the string and r be the maximum position of “<”:

  • If one of those characters doesn’t exist, then the only result is 0. An empty magical string.

  • If they exist but l > r, it means we have a string like <<<<<>>>>, result is also 0.

  • Else we should always use the characters S[l] and S[r] for the limits of the magical string. Maybe the characters between them can contain more valid characters. We can count the maximum solution by calling f(S’) on the remaining characters between l and r. The result is 2 + f(S’)

not in S:

# Find extreme > and <:
  left = min(i
    for i in range(len(S)) if S[i] == '>')
right = max(i
  for i in range(len(S)) if S[i] == '<')
#
if those exist, result is 2 + f(remaining string)
if left < right:
  return 2 + self.getLongest(S[left + 1: right])
else:
  return 0

A linear-time approach

One way to reach linear time is to notice that the previous approach can be implemented iteratively, making sure the pointers l and r never overlap and that guarantees O(n) execution time:

int getLongest(string S) {
  int n = S.size();
  int i = 0, j = n - 1;
  int res = 0;
  while (i < j) {
    while ((i < j) && (S[i] != '>')) {
      i++;
    }
    while ((j > i) && (S[j] != '<')) {
      j--;
    }
    if (i < j) {
      res += 2;
    }
    i++;
    j--;
  }
  return res;
}

High Scorepieguy for 479.03 points (5 mins 59 secs)

The key idea is to do things backwards than we did in the algorithm explained for the division 2 version. Deciding the “normal” sets before the variety sets.

Optimal to take K balls

Let’s say there are t balls of a color. If t > K, we can make a set of K balls of that color. What we should notice is that this is always an optimal decision:

It won’t affect your ability to make normal sets of other colors.

The important question is whether it would be more convenient to use some of the K balls in “variety” sets. First of all, it would have to be K other variety sets. If less than K of the balls were used in variety sets, the remaining balls would still need to be used in a normal set, thus the number of used sets would be worse than if we just used a single normal set.

We will use a proof by contradiction. Assume using the balls of this color (Let’s call it color 0) in K variety sets was better than using these balls in a normal set. In order for these K sets to be a better sequence of moves than the single set, they would need to take balls of at least other K colors. If the number of colors is x <= K, we could instead make x normal sets, one for each color and we would be using a same or better number of sets as with K variety sets. And in this problem, K is the maximum number of colors.

Division

Let’s then repeat the following operation: For each color, take a set of K balls of the same color until the remaining number of balls of color is less than K and count the number of sets we make. This is the same as the division algorithm: So for t balls of a given color, the number of normal sets we can make is t/K (integer division) and the remaining balls after making those sets is t mod %K.

We repeat this for each ball color. Then we just need to find the minimum number of sets for the remaining balls.

The remaining balls

Many balls may remain, the number of remaining balls of each color will be strictly less than K. In this sub-problem, we need to find the minimum number of sets.

We will take a page from the division 2 version. If we fix the number of variety sets v, then we can calculate the number of normal sets. The division 2 approach needs to be updated to consider that there can be 100000 colors and that each color has less than K balls. This time, for each color such that its number of balls is greater than v, we need a normal set. Therefore, the result is: (“number of colors with more balls than v”) + v).

Because of the formula, the relevant values of v that could be optimal are those such that v = X[i] for a given i. Thanks to this, we have only O(K) values of v to try. Also, if the list of remaining balls is sorted, it is easy to know the number of colors with more balls than v.

/* assume we already generated the X vector using the random algorithm *
int minPacks(int K, vector<int> &X)
{
    int sum = 0;
    for (int &y: X) {
        sum += y / K;
        y %= K;
    }
    int res = sum + K;
    
    sort(X.begin(), X.end());
    for (int i = 0; i < K; i++) {
        // try v = X[i]
        // K - (i + 1) is the number of colors with more balls than v:
        res = std::min(res, sum + X[i] + K - (i + 1) ); 
    }
    return res;
}

LotteryTree

Problem Details

Used as: Division One - Level Three:

Value1000
Submission Rate15 / 779 (1.93%)
Success Rate3 / 15 (20.00%)
High Scorecgy4ever for 541.37 points (32 mins 42 secs)
Average Score440.07 (for 3 correct submissions)

Leaf probabilities

The algorithm that picks the winning number does not depend directly on how we draw the tree. What matters is the numbers written on each leaf. It turns out that for each leaf, there is a probability that the number in that leaf will be picked. Starting at the root, there is a 1 / d_0 probability that the sub-tree containing the leaf is picked, where d_0 is the number of children of the root. Assume this sub-tree’s root had d_1 children, then there is a 1 / d_1 probability to pick the correct sub-subtree. And so and so. The probability p_i to pick a leaf i is equal to 1 divided by the product of each vertex degrees in the path between the root and leaf.

These probabilities are independent of how we draw the tree. How do they affect the decision?

The bottom edge

After drawing the tree, we split the bottom edge in P segments, each with a integer i on them. Assigning integer i to a segment means assigning i to all the leaves inside the segment. For each leaf j, if leaf j is in segment i, it adds p_j to the probability that integer i is picked. The objective of the problem is that the distribution of probabilities to pick each integer i is uniform. Which means that the total sum sum(p_j) of leaves j assigned to edge i must be the same for all i.

A great way to visualize this is to interpret the bottom edge as a sum of probabilities. The bottom edge measures 1.0 in total. Each of the segments we pick should measure 1 / P in total. Then come the leaves, each leaf i should measure p_i in the bottom.

Since the segments that split the bottom edge cannot contain a leaf partially, then the segments’ ends should match 0, 1 or the end of a leaf’s segments. If we ensure that the segments have equal length, then we can be certain the probability will be uniformly distributed. This teaches us how to draw the tree:

If we see the leaves as segments of length p_i then the problem becomes all about the ordering we use for the leaves. If the ordering makes it so all 1 / P segments’ ends coincide with 0, 1 or the ends of a leaf, then the tree can be drawn in a valid way. The order of the leaves is determined by how we choose to draw the tree.

In the image, we are given the tree in the top as input to do a lottery between three people. The two bottom images are ways to draw the tree, but only the one in the right is correct, because the 1/P divisions match the leaf limits. The way in which you draw the tree, determines the order in which the leaves appear in the bottom segment. The problem is all about picking this order. So answer the question: Is it possible to put the leaves in an order that makes the 1/P segments match the leaves?

Parent segments

We can see non-leaf nodes as segments too.

The length of a node’s segment is equal to the sum of the segments of its children. If it is a root, it is equal to p_i as described above.
This is useful because we can find an optimal substructure. The main problem can be seen as: "Can we correctly fit the root’s segment at [0, 1] ? Then we will have many similar sub-problems of the type: "Can we fit a node with segment length W at [X, X+W] ?

Maximum bipartite matching

All children of a node will have the same segment length. If the node has degree g, there will be g sub-segments of [X, X+W] where we can place each of its children. There are g children. For each pair (i,j) where i is a child and j is a sub-segment, we have to ask the question: "Is it possible to put the equivalent segment of node i in the sub-segment: [X + j*W/g, X + (j+1)*W/g] assume we already know the answers to those questions. What we have now is a list of g nodes , g spaces and a matrix that tells us if it is possible to place each node i in space j. We need to answer if it is possible to pick an ordering in which each i will be placed in a different space. This is just a maximum bipartite matching problem.

Dynamic programming

There is one more thing. Each subproblem of the kind: "Is it possible to put node i in interval [a,b] requires us to call g new instances of the sub-problem and do a bipartite matching. We need to estimate how many instances of this problem can there be. Without further analysis, the number of instances can be very large.

A important observation is that the number of intervals for each node i is limited.

  • The root will only be given interval [0,1].

  • If the root has g children, then the nodes that are children of the root will always have the candidates: [0/g,1/g], [1/g, 2/g], … [(g-1)/g, g/g].

  • Now imagine one of the children x of the root has g_1 children. If we pick [0/g,1/g] for this node, the children will have candidates: [0/(gg_1),1/(gg_1)], …, ]. If instead [1/g,2/g] was picked, the candidates for the children will be: [g_1/(gg_1)],…. And so and so. You shall notice that the total candidates for its children will be: [0/(gg_1),1/(gg_1)],[1/(gg_1),2/(gg_1)],…[(gg_1-1)/(gg_1),(gg_1)/(gg_1)].

  • Remember that 1/g will be the length of the root’s children and 1_(gg_1) the length of x's children. Can we generalize it? For each node with a segment of length w, the tried intervals will be: [0*w, 1*w],[1*w, 2*w],…[(t-1)*w, t*w]; where t is the number of children of the node. We can prove this by induction. It is true for the root, and if it is true for a node with distance k to the root, it should also be true for a root with distance k+1 to the root.

Another observation here is that many combinations of i and [a,b] are trivially solved. Imagine that there was no j such that a <= j / P <= b. This would mean that none of the 1/P divisions lies in the interval [a,b] and that any permutation of leaves in the sub-tree would be valid.

If we put the two observations together, we have that the only important problems we need to solve are those in which a node i with length w is tried against an interval [j*w, (j+1)*w] that contains at least one k/P where k is a integer. For each integer k, there is at most one interval [j*w, (j+1)*w] that contains k/P. This means that for each node, there are O(k) intervals that are non-trivial to solve. If we use dynamic programming, we will need to perform the bipartite matching O(nk) times.

MaxFlow::network flowSolver; // a maxflow solver class:

vector < int > children[101];
int P;
map < int, int > mem[101];
// for memoization, we use an associative array. mem[i][j] describes the
// entry for node i. Where j is the number of sub-interval of length w
// the number of sub-interval can be pretty large, hence why we do not
// use a simple array. Note each mem[i] will need at most P values in the map<int,int>

bool isItPossible(int i, int x, int j) {
  assert(mem[i].size() <= P);

  // interval length w = 1 / x
  // Can we put node i in interval [j/x , (j+1)/x] ?
  if (mem[i].count(j) == 0) {
    bool res = false;
    if (children[i].size() == 0) {
      // A leaf.
      // make sure there is no integer k such that k/p is strictly inside interval
      res = true;
      for (int k = 1; k < P; k++) {
        // Check if ( j/x < k/P < (j+1)/x):
        if (P * j < x * k && x * k < P * (j + 1)) {
          res = false;
        }
      }
    } else {

      int g = children[i].size();
      bool can[g][g];
      memset(can, 1, sizeof(can));
      for (int b = 0; b < g; b++) {
        bool nontrivial = false;
        // We want to know if a k exists such that k/P is between
        // the b-th sub-interval
        for (int k = 1; k < P; k++) {
          // the b-th sub-interval starts at j/x  + b /gx
          // and finishes at j/x + (b+1)/gx
          // (j/x is the start of node i's interval)

          // Check if ( j/x + b/gx < k/P < j/x + (b+1)/gx:  //gPx
          //          j*g*P + b*P  <k*g*x< j*g*P+(b+1)*P 
          if (g * j * P + b * P < g * k * x && g * k * x < g * j * P + (b + 1) * P) {
            nontrivial = true;
          }

        }
        if (nontrivial) {
          for (int a = 0; a < g; a++) {
            can[a][b] = isItPossible(children[i][a], x * g, j * g + b);
          }
        } else {
          for (int a = 0; a < g; a++) {
            can[a][b] = true;
          }
        }
      }
      // Now we call a maxflow solver to find the maximum bipartite
      // matching making use of the can[][] matrix:
      flowSolver.reset();
      for (int a = 0; a < 2 * g; a++) {
        flowSolver.addVertex();
      }
      flowSolver.source = flowSolver.addVertex();
      flowSolver.sink = flowSolver.addVertex();
      for (int a = 0; a < g; a++) {
        flowSolver.addEdge(flowSolver.source, a, 1);
        flowSolver.addEdge(g + a, flowSolver.sink, 1);
      }
      for (int a = 0; a < g; a++) {
        for (int b = 0; b < g; b++) {
          if (can[a][b]) {
            flowSolver.addEdge(a, g + b, 1);
          }
        }
      }
      // If the maximum flow is g, the maximum matching is g and there
      // exists a valid ordering:
      res = (flowSolver.maxFlow() == g);
    }
    mem[i][j] = res;

  }
  return mem[i][j];
}

string isFairTree(vector < int > tree, int P) {
  this - > P = P;
  for (int i = 0; i < tree.size(); i++) {
    children[tree[i]].push_back(i + 1);
  }
  return isItPossible(0, 1, 0) ? "YES" : "NO";
}

DivideByZero

Problem Details

Used as: Division Two - Level One:

Value250
Submission Rate643 / 776 (82.86%)
Success Rate441 / 643 (68.58%)
High Scoreoilover for 249.94 points (0 mins 27 secs)
Average Score183.70 (for 441 correct submissions)

This is a simulation problem. Notice that only the numbers from 1 to 100 are allowed as input. Whenever you take the integer part of division a/b where a > b, the integer part will be greater than 0 and not larger than a. It follows that in the worst case, the largest list of numbers we can reach is the one containing all 100 numbers from 1 to 100. So the list will never contain more than 100 numbers. This means that we can just use the straightforward simulation.

More formally: We will add at most 98 new numbers and for each of them, we need an O(n^2) search to browse through each of the pairs of those numbers. We also need an O(n) search to verify that a/b does not exist. This means that in an exaggerated worst case, we need O(N^4) steps, where N is the maximum number in the sequence. Even that slow solution should run within the time limit for N <= 100 in TC’s server. Like in the following python code:

class DivideByZero:
  def CountNumbers(self, numbers):
  s = list(numbers)
old = 0
# repeat as long as there are new numbers in the list:
  while len(s) > old:
  old = len(s) # save current amount of numbers in the paper.
# find a good pair(a, b)
for a in s:
  for b in s:
  if a > b and a / b not in s:
  # A new number, append it.
s.append(a / b)
return len(s)

Note that problem called DivideByZero, but the division of the two numbers cannot be 0 because a > b, so a division by zero will simply not happen.

There are many ways to simplify the code and also make the algorithm faster. In the following c++ solution, we use a set data structure from its library to keep track of the numbers in the list. This makes searching for the number in the paper and adding a new number to the paper O(log(n)), thus simplifying complexity to O(N^3 log(N)):

int CountNumbers(vector < int > numbers) {
  int old = 0;
  set < int > paper(numbers.begin(), numbers.end());
  while (paper.size() > old) {
    old = paper.size();
    for (int a: paper) {
      for (int b: paper) {
        if ((a > b) && (paper.count(a / b) == 0)) {
          paper.insert(a / b);
        }
      }
    }
  }
  return paper.size();
}

class DivideByZero:

old = 0
# repeat as long as there are new numbers in the list:

for each a in a copy of the set:

s.add(a / b)

A possible issue with any implementation is that we have to add elements to the paper set/list/vector while iterating through it. Depending on the language/data structure used that might not be possible. In the previous example, we make copies of the set to iterate it so we can modify it freely.

Consider that even with n <= 100 (Where n is the maximum dimension of the matrix), a complexity of O(n^4) shall work all right within the time limit for most of the supported languages. We’ll begin with a relatively simple O(n^4) approach that can also be turned into O(n^3) with a small change.

Fix the first and last column

The rectangle we have to search for can be described by two corners: (x_0,y_0), (x_1,y_1), what we can do is fix the start and final columns, the x_0 = i and x_1 = j coordinates. Then we are interested in finding the maximum height of a correct subrectangle inside the matrix composed of columns between x_0 = i and x_1 = j, inclusive. For each pair (i,j), we can ignore the other columns.

Each of the sub-rows should be of the kind 010101… or 1010101… with an O(n^2) we can find whether each of these sub-rows belongs to either of those types. We can flag them as type ‘0’ for 010101… , type ‘1’ for 10101010… and we should also flag ‘x’ to the rows that don’t belong to any of those types.

After that we have the flags of each of the rows as a sequence like “010XX10101X11000X110101XXX”. We should just find the largest substring of this sequence that is made of alternating 1s and 0s. The size of this largest substring is equal to the largest height of a correct sub-rectangle within the two columns i, j. We can find that largest substring with an O(n^2) loop but O(n) is also easy.
In total the following code will need O(n^4) steps:

int MaxArea(vector < string > board) {
  int res = 0, w = board[0].size(), h = board.size();
  // fix columns i and j:
  for (int i = 0; i < w; i++) {
    for (int j = i; j < w; j++) {
      // the flags for each row:
      char row[h];

      // find the flags, 0 for rows 010101.., 1 for rows 101010... X for other.
      for (int k = 0; k < h; k++) {
        bool alt = true;
        for (int r = i + 1; r <= j; r++) {
          alt = alt && (board[k][r] != board[k][r - 1]);
        }
        row[k] = (alt ? board[k][i] : 'X');
      }

      // Find the largest substring of alternating 0s and 1s:
      int mh = 0, ch = 0;
      for (int k = 0; k < h; k++) {
        if (row[k] == 'X') {
          ch = 0;
        } else if ((ch > 0) && (row[k - 1] != row[k])) {
          ch++;
        } else {
          ch = 1;
        }
        mh = max(mh, ch);
      }
      // The largest height is mh, the width is j - i +1
      res = std::max(res, mh * (j - i + 1));
    }
  }
  return res;
}

Cubic complexity

The reason the previous code is O(n^4) is that we need an O(n^2) loop to flag the rows. We can optimize to O(n^3) by noticing that the flags for j can be found in O(n) if we reuse the knowledge of the flags for j-1. For each row k, if the flag for j-1 was “X”, the flag will also be “X”, else if the bit at position (k , j) is different to the bit at position (k, j-1) the flag can stay the same (the first character of the sequence is the one at column i).

int MaxArea(vector < string > board) {
  int res = 0, w = board[0].size(), h = board.size();
  // fix columns i and j:
  for (int i = 0; i < w; i++) {
    // the flags for each row:
    char row[h];
    for (int j = i; j < w; j++) {

      if (j == i) { //initialize flags:
        for (int k = 0; k < h; k++) {
          row[k] = board[k][i];
        }
      } else {
        // update the flags, 0 for rows 010101.., 1 for rows 101010... X for other.
        for (int k = 0; k < h; k++) {
          if (board[k][j] == board[k][j - 1]) {
            row[k] = 'X';
          }
        }
      }

      // Find the largest substring of alternating 0s and 1s:
      int mh = 0, ch = 0;
      for (int k = 0; k < h; k++) {
        if (row[k] == 'X') {
          ch = 0;
        } else if ((ch > 0) && (row[k - 1] != row[k])) {
          ch++;
        } else {
          ch = 1;
        }
        mh = max(mh, ch);
      }
      // The largest height is mh, the width is j - i +1
      res = std::max(res, mh * (j - i + 1));
    }
  }
  return res;
}

MiningGoldEasy

Problem Details

Value1000

Success Rate3 / 13 (23.08%)
High ScoreHasan0540 for 606.97 points (26 mins 50 secs)

A recurrence

Let’s try to solve the problem using a recursive logic. First of all, since before the first step we can travel to any of the possible locations, we should find the top score you can earn if we move to location (x,y) before the first move. We should try each location (x,y) as a candidate.

After the first move, you earn some gold depending on the location of event 0. Then you can choose to move to a new point (x’,y’) which shares column or row (x,y). After this move, we are in a similar sub-problem to the initial one, you are located at position (x’,y’) and want to know the maximum earnings you can make with the last K-1 events. (Where K is the number of events).

The recurrence relation goes as follows: f(x,y,i) finds the maximum earnings if you are located at position (x,y) before event i.

  • If i = K, all events have finished and you cannot earn any more gold. The result is 0. This is the base case.

  • Else we earn some gold m equal to the Manhattan distance between (x,y) and the event’s location. Finally you can move to a new point (x’,y’) and then the remaining events will happen and you might move again later. We can try to iterate through all reachable points (x’,y’) that share the column or row with (x,y) and pick the maximum f(x’,y’,i+1).

The complexity of this approach is large. Imagine T was the largest dimension. There are O(T^2) values for (x,y) , which means there are O(KT^2) possible states for the function. In each state, we need an O(T) loop to pick the new row or the new column. In total we have an O(KT^3) complexity. Note that T can be very large: T '= 1000000.

Relevant points

In order to make the solution more efficient, we just need to notice that it is never necessary to move to a point (x,y) such that x or y don’t match at least one of the events’ coordinates. This means that there are only O(k) options we need to try for x and y in each step and there are O(k^2) different values. This changes the complexity to O(k^4).

int t, N, M;
int mem[50][50][51];
vector < int > event_i, event_j;

int f(int a, int b, int s) {
  int & res = mem[a][b][s];
  if (res == -1) {
    if (s == t) {
      res = 0;
    } else {
      int x = N + M -
        abs(event_i[a] - event_i[s]) -
        abs(event_j[b] - event_j[s]);
      res = 0;
      // change row:
      for (int na = 0; na < event_i.size(); na++) {
        res = std::max(res, x + f(na, b, s + 1));
      }
      // change column:
      for (int nb = 0; nb < event_j.size(); nb++) {
        res = std::max(res, x + f(a, nb, s + 1));
      }
    }
  }
  return res;
}

int GetMaximumGold(int N, int M, vector < int > event_i, vector < int > event_j) {
  this - > t = event_i.size();
  this - > N = N;
  this - > M = M;
  this - > event_i = event_i;
  this - > event_j = event_j;

  memset(mem, -1, sizeof(mem));
  int mx = 0;
  for (int a = 0; a < event_i.size(); a++) {
    for (int b = 0; b < event_j.size(); b++) {
      mx = std::max(mx, f(a, b, 0));
    }
  }
  return mx;
}

AlbertoTheAviator

High Scorepashka for 525.21 points (6 mins 13 secs)
Average Score326.62 (for 137 correct submissions)

The selection and the order

We are searching for the maximum-size set of missions that can be done. Let’s try a simpler problem first: Given a fixed set S of missions, can we do all the missions in that set?

We just need to find an optimal way to order the missions. This would mean that if the ordering doesn’t allow us to do all the missions we would be certain it is impossible to do it. There can be many orderings for missions and we will leave that analysis for later. For now, let’s assume that ordering exists.

Dynamic programming

Assuming that ordering exists, we cannot just iterate through all (at most 2^50) subsets of missions and remember the largest set that can be completed. However, we can use dynamic programming.

The ordering must have established a mission such that, if it is in the set we picked, should be solved before all the other missions. Let’s call this mission, mission 0. Initially we have two options for the set: Either include mission 0 or not.

  • If we decide not to include it, we can just focus on the remaining missions.

  • If we decide to include it, we can assume it will be used first. This means that for the remaining missions we pick , the fuel value will be modified and we can proceed to decide the remaining missions…

The recurrence relation idea is as follows: G( F, i ), we have already processed the i missions that, according to the ordering, should be finished before the other missions. The current fuel amount is F. We need to decide what to do with the i-th mission (according to the ordering).

  • If it is possible to do it (F is large enough), then the fuel amount will decrease and then possibly increase to a value F’. The result will be: 1 + G(F’, i+1).

  • If we cannot or decide not to do the i-th mission, the result will be: G(F, i+1).

  • The maximum between 1 + G(F’, i+1) and G(F, i+1), is the result for G(F, x).

The amount of fuel is at most 5000, each recursion step is O(1). If we use memoization the complexity is: O(F * k).

The best ordering

Let’s now talk about the best order to take the missions. There are many suspects: Sort by fuel mission cost? Fuel cost - refuel cost? Just refuel cost? There is no solution to this dilemma other than try them all until we can find one that we can prove to be optimal.

It turns out that the best ordering is to order the sequence by refuel in non-increasing order. How can we prove it? There are a couple of example proofs in the forums.

const int MAX = 52;
static
const int MM = 5002;

int n;
vector < int > ids, duration, refuel;
int mem[MAX][MM];

int recurse(int idx, int rem) {
  if (idx >= n) {
    return 0;
  }
  if (mem[idx][rem] == -1) {
    int ans = 0;
    // Don't take current mission
    ans = max(ans, recurse(idx + 1, rem));
    // Take current mission
    if (rem >= duration[ids[idx]]) {
      ans = max(ans, recurse(idx + 1, rem - duration[ids[idx]] + refuel[ids[idx]]) + 1);
    }
    mem[idx][rem] = ans;
  }
  return mem[idx][rem];
}

int MaximumFlights(int F, vector < int > _duration, vector < int > _refuel) {
  n = (int) _duration.size();
  ids.resize(n);
  duration = _duration;
  refuel = _refuel;

  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    ids[i] = i;
  }
  // sort non-decreasingly by fuel:
  sort(ids.begin(), ids.end(), [ & ](int i, int j) {
    return refuel[i] > refuel[j];
  });
  memset(mem, -1, sizeof(mem));
  return recurse(0, F);
}

MiningGoldHard

Problem Details

Used as: Division One - Level Three:

Value900
Submission Rate21 / 524 (4.01%)
Success Rate6 / 21 (28.57%)
High ScoreSkidanovAlex for 517.57 points (29 mins 29 secs)
Average Score434.86 (for 6 correct submissions)

Independent axis

In each event, the amount of gold you make depends on the x and y coordinates of your current cell. The cost function is: N + M - |e_i - x| - |e_j - y|, where (e_i, e_j) are the event coordinates. Then the allowed movement is, you can move at most d_i units vertically and d_j units horizontally. It turns out that the solution is independent for each axis/direction. Just consider the values: N - |e_i - x| and M - |e_j - y| as independent parts of the total profit. Then we are allowed to make decisions in each axis without affecting the other.

Let’s then just solve for a single axis. There are points x=0, 1, … N. Before the first event, you can pick any of those points. After an event i with event position e_i, you earn N - |e_i-x| and you can increase or decrease x by at most d_i units. Without leaving interval [0,N]. If we can solve this sub-problem, we can use the same solution to solve the sub-problem for y and M.

Dynamic programming

There is a slow dynamic programming approach that can be used as a good starting point. Imagine we knew, for each x, the value f(x,i), the maximum earnings you can make if you are located at point x starting at move i. The dynamic programming is simple:

  • If i = k (Where k is the number of events), then there is nothing to do anymore and the maximum earning is 0 (base case).

  • Else we earn N - | e_i - x| gold and then we need to decide where to move. We should pick a value x’ such that f(x’, i+1) is as large as possible. Then the overall result is: N - | e_i - x | + f(x’ , i+1). Note that x’ follows is a integer that follows some constraints: (0 <= x’ <= N), ( |x - x’| <= d_i).

This dynamic programming approach has a problem: The values of x are between 0 and N, inclusive, and N can be very large. In order to turn this knowledge into a solution, we needs a very good idea:

Function graphics

If you think about it, f(x,i) can be seen as describing a function dependent on x: F_i(x). If we take this perspective, then we should try to analyze the graphics of each F_i.

We know that F_k(x) = f(x,k) = 0, the graphic will just be a straight horizontal line.
The graphic for F_(k-1)(x) shall be more interesting. F_(k-1)(x) = |e_(k-1) - x| + 0 = |e_(k-1) - x|. Graphics of functions with absolute values work as follows:

So we can predict function F_(k-1) will be simple. How about F_(k-2)? F_(k-2)(x) requires us to find the maximum F_(k-1)(x’) such that |x’ - x| <= d. Then we add |e_(k-2) - x|, we can do it in parts. How about we define g(x) = max( F_(k-1)(x’) ) this new function will have a graphic that is based on the graphic of: F_(k-1)(x).

To this function, we add |e_(k-2) - x|, which is another absolute value function. That function and the resulting addition will look like this:

The nice thing is that now, we can use that addition as the graphic for F_(k-2)(x). We can repeat this process for smaller k. For example, with d_(k-3) = 1, the max( F_(k-2)(x’) : |x - x’| <= d_(k-3)), function can be found using a similar logic as before:

The idea of this problem is to repeat these processes until we find F_0(x) and then it would be easy to pick the x with the maximum F_0(x) so we can pick it as the starting location and solve the problem. How can we do it efficiently?

Expand

The first operation we’ll consider is the one in which we find the function g(x) = max(F_i(x’) : |x’ - x| <= d). We had a simple example in the second image and a more complicated example in the fourth:

In both cases, the logic used is the same. We find the maximum point of the function and then we shift the left-side of the function left and the right side of the function right - we expand the function. There are many details in this one. First of all, is it always correct?

Always concave

This approach should be true whenever the left side of the function is ascending from left to right and decreasing from right to left. A interval in which all values of f(x) are equal to the maximum is also allowed. We are describing a concave function. Certainly, if the function is concave (Its negative is convex), this approach works. Can we prove that the functions will always be concave before we expand them?

Initially, the function is a straight line equal to y = 0. Then for each i, we repeat the two processes: Expand and add. The expand operation requires the function to be concave. If the function is concave, it will remain concave after the expand operation. Then we add some |x - e| to the function. All functions in the form of |x-e| are concave. The sum of two concave functions is also concave. This means that after every step, the function will remain concave.

Polyline

How can we implement the expansion? An easy way is to consider the function as a polyline. The significant vertices are those in which the direction changes. The maximum y will be in one of these vertices. After we find the maximum y we can offset the x values of the vertices to the left and to the right of this top vertex. We also need to create a new vertex to duplicate the top value. In total we need O(t) steps to do this, where t is the number of vertices in the initial polyline.

Cut

An issue is that the expansion will usually move vertices of the polyline outside the (0 <= x <= N) interval. Those values of x would be invalid. We need to discard those, but we need the y values for x = 0 and N=0, we might need to do interpolation to find them.

Add

The addition process is also easy when we see the function as a polyline.

The y value in each segment in the polyline increases by some |e - x|. We might also need to add a new significant vertex to the polyline, the one where x = e. If x = e was not a significant vertex in this polyline before the addition, we need to find its y value using interpolation.

Complexity and code

Each of the operations is O(t) where t is the number of vertices currently in the polyline. Operations usually add new vertices. Each expansion and add operation will increase the number of significant vertices by at most 1. Initially, there are 2 vertices. It follows that the number of significant vertices is O(k), because we repeat the two processes k times. The total complexity is O(k^2), which is good enough for topcoder’s servers because (k <= 1000)

int N;
vector < int > x, y;

// Get py for a point (px,py) in segment (x0,y0)-(x1,y1).
int interpolate(long x0, long y0, long x1, long y1, long px) {
  return (int)(y0 + (px - x0) * (y1 - y0) / (x1 - x0));
}

// The "expand" operation.
// We have a function of pairs (x, dp[i+1][x])
// We want to find a function (x, max( dp[i+1][x1] : abs(x - x1) <= d) )
// Which translates into finding the maximum dp[i+1][x] and "expanding"
// its value d units left and d units right.
void expand(int d) {
  // Find the index bi with maximum y[]. The set of x with maximum y[] will
  // be an interval.
  int bi = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < x.size(); i++) {
    if (y[i] > y[bi]) {
      bi = i;
    }
  }
  vector < int > nx, ny;

  // Function point(px,py) describes what to do in case we find a point (px,py) :
  auto point = [ & ](int px, int py) {
    nx.push_back(px);
    ny.push_back(py);
  };
  // Move smaller points d units left
  for (int i = 0; i <= bi; i++) {
    point(x[i] - d, y[i]);
  }
  // *Copy* bi d units right..
  if (d != 0) { //a corner case, when d = 0, don't add a point.
    point(x[bi] + d, y[bi]);
  }
  // Move larger points d units left
  for (int i = bi + 1; i < x.size(); i++) {
    point(x[i] + d, y[i]);
  }
  x = nx;
  y = ny;
}

// After expand() operations, we might need to cut the values of the function
// for x < 0 or x > N.    
void cut() {
  vector < int > nx, ny;
  // function point(px,py) to add a point to the list whenever we find it:
  auto point = [ & ](int px, int py) {
    nx.push_back(px);
    ny.push_back(py);
  };

  for (int i = 0; i < x.size(); i++) {
    // x[i] within bounds, include this point.
    if (0 <= x[i] && x[i] <= N) {
      point(x[i], y[i]);
    }
    // If 0 was between x[i] and x[i+1], it means we need to cut the function
    // We interpolate to find the value of y for x=0.
    if ((i + 1 < x.size()) && (x[i] < 0 && 0 < x[i + 1])) {
      point(0, interpolate(x[i], y[i], x[i + 1], y[i + 1], 0));
    }
    // Same with x = N
    if ((i + 1 < x.size()) && (x[i] < N && N < x[i + 1])) {
      point(N, interpolate(x[i], y[i], x[i + 1], y[i + 1], N));
    }
  }
  x = nx;
  y = ny;
}

// The "add" operation.
// We want to create a function of pairs (x, dp[i][x])
// We already know the maximum (dp[i][x1] : abs(x1 - x) <= d) for each x.
// (The maximum score we can get *after* event i if we start event i at point x.
// To each of those maximums add N + abs(e - px). 
void add(int e) {
  vector < int > nx, ny;
  // function to add abs(e - px) to each point (px,py) we find in function:
  auto point = [ & ](int px, int py) {
    nx.push_back(px);
    ny.push_back(py + N - abs(e - px));
  };

  // We should add N + abs(e - px) to all points in the function.
  // Also need to include point x = e, if it doesn't already exist.
  for (int i = 0; i < x.size(); i++) {
    point(x[i], y[i]);
    // If x = e is not in the function, use interpolation to find its
    // y value and add it:
    if ((i + 1 < x.size()) && (x[i] < e && e < x[i + 1])) {
      point(e, interpolate(x[i], y[i], x[i + 1], y[i + 1], e));
    }
  }
  x = nx;
  y = ny;
}

// Solves for x:
int getMaximumGoldX(int N, vector < int > e, vector < int > d) {

  for (int i = e.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
    add(e[i]);
    if (i > 0) {
      expand(d[i - 1]);
      cut();
    }
    cout << i << endl;

  return * max_element(y.begin(), y.end());
}

int GetMaximumGold(int N, int M, vector < int > event_i, vector < int > event_j, vector < int > event_di, vector < int > event_dj) {
  return getMaximumGoldX(N, event_i, event_di) +
    getMaximumGoldX(M, event_j, event_dj);
}

This is an implementation problem: As the problem statement says: “For each D between 0 and 9, inclusive, X either does not contain the digit D at all, or it contains exactly two digits D, and there are precisely D other digits between them.”. We just need to iterate through digits from 0 to 9 and find their positions. If the number of positions for at least one digit is not two or zero, then the number is not interesting. If the number of positions is 2, and the distance between the positions is not equal to D, then it is invalid as well: If the two positions are p_0 and p_1, then p_1 must be equal to p_0 + D + 1, to ensure that there are exactly D digits between them.

string isInteresting(string x) {
  for (int d = 0; d < 10; d++) {
    vector < int > p; //list of positions equal to d
    for (int i = 0; i < x.size(); i++) {
      if (x[i] == '0' + d) {
        p.push_back(i);
      }
    }
    // if digit doesn't exist in x, it is already valid.
    if (p.size() == 0) {
      continue;
    }
    if (p.size() == 2) {
      if (p[1] != p[0] + d + 1) {
        return "Not interesting";
      }
    } else {
      return "Not interesting";
    }
  }
  return "Interesting";
}

class InterestingNumber:
  def isInteresting(self, x):
  for d in range(10):
  ch = chr(d + ord('0'))
#
if ch is not in x, then there are zero positions and it is valid
if ch in x:
  # find the indexes that contain ch:
  indexes = [i
    for i in range(len(x)) if x[i] == ch]
# number of positions must be 2, and the distance should be:
  if len(indexes) != 2 or indexes[1] - indexes[0] - 1 != d:
  return "Not interesting"
return "Interesting"

LCMSetEasy

Problem Details

Used as: Division Two - Level Two:

Value500
Submission Rate480 / 941 (51.01%)
Success Rate169 / 480 (35.21%)
High Scorebakusou for 490.19 points (4 mins 2 secs)

There can be at most 50 elements in set S, so we need a good strategy to pick the subset of elements that we want to take part in the LCM.

Elements we cannot use

Consider any elements y of S such that x is not a multiple of y. If we take the LCM of y and any group of numbers, x will never be a multiple of the LCM. For example x = 128, y = 3, any multiple of 3 will not divide x evenly.

A formal proof: If x is not a multiple of y, no multiple of y will divide x evenly: By contradiction, assume a integer k > 0 exists such that x is a multiple of k*y: That is: \exists k_2: x = k_2* k* y. However, this means there exists a k_3 = k_2*k such that: x = k_3 * y, which would mean that x is a multiple of y and contradict our hypothesis.

Elements we should use

We can discard any element that does not divide x. What about the remaining elements? What about we take the LCM of them all? We know that x is a multiple of all of these numbers. The least common multiple of those numbers will be, by definition a common multiple of those numbers that is less than or equal to x. If the LCM is less than x, even though we used all the numbers in the set that are divisors of x, then we can be certain it is impossible to find a subset that will give x as the LCM. If the LCM is x, then we have found a valid subset.

Solution summary and code

As usual, we can use the Euclidean algorithm to quickly calculate the Greatest-common-divisor. Then we use the formula lcm(a,b) = (ab)/gcd(a,b) to calculate the least common multiple.

Find the LCM of all numbers y in the set that are divisors of x. The answer is “Possible” if and only if this LCM is equal to x.

int gcd(int a, int b) {
  while (b != 0) {
    int c = a;
    a = b;
    b = c % a;
  }
  return a;
}

string include(vector < int > S, int x) {
  int lcm = 1;
  for (int y: S) {
    if (x % y == 0) {
      lcm = lcm / gcd(lcm, y) * y;
    }
  }
  return (lcm == x) ? "Possible" : "Impossible";
}

def div2solution(S, x):
  return x == reduce(lcm, [z
    for z in S
    if (x % z == 0)], 1)

class LCMSetEasy:
  def include(self, S, x):
  return ["Impossible", "Possible"][div2solution(S, x)]

def gcd(a, b):
  (b, a) = sorted((a, b))
while b != 0:
  (a, b) = (b, a % b)
return a

def lcm(a, b):
  return a * b / gcd(a, b)

ElephantDrinkingEasy

Problem Details

Used as: Division Two - Level Three:

Value1000
Submission Rate102 / 941 (10.84%)
Success Rate17 / 102 (16.67%)
High ScoreSnokas for 748.32 points (17 mins 46 secs)
Average Score517.80 (for 17 correct submissions)

The important thing is to notice that the map will be very small, at most 5 rows and 5 columns. The complicating part of designing a strategy for this problem is the conflict between columns and rows. So making an Elephant drink from the left side would possibly block the chances of other elephants to drink from the top or the bottom. The idea is that we can exploit the small dimensions of the map by noticing that there will be at most 10 horizontal positions where we can locate elephants (5 top and 5 bottom).

Vertical drinking

There will be 2^(2n) number of subsets of horizontal positions where we can place elephants. 2^10 = 1024 is rather small, so we can just try all these possibilities.

Each of the elephants picked to be placed horizontally should attempt to drink something:

An optimal strategy should be to make each elephant at the top try the water pond in its row that is closest to the top. Each elephant at the bottom should try the water pond that is closest to the bottom. This should maximize the chances to add elephants to the vertical positions, because this keeps the each of the trunks as short as possible.

Maybe there are not enough ponds for the elephants in a row, then the subset of horizontal positions was just invalid and we can ignore it.

For each of the elephants placed horizontally, we need to find the closest pond and also mark the squares between the elephant and the pond as impassable by horizontal trunks.

Horizontal drinking

There are some vertical trunks in the map and they block the way for horizontal trunks. A good way to picture this is as if we marked the map with some special characters to represent that the cells are impassable by horizontal trunks. We need to maximize the number of elephants placed at the vertical positions that can drink from a pond. This time we should try each of the vertical positions and try to make the elephant drink the closest pond. If the way is not blocked, the elephant can and should drink it.

Implementation

The top level logic of the solution is simple: For each set of positions of elephants in the top and bottom row: Make each of those elephants drink the closest pond in its row. Then, for each elephant in the left-most and right-most column, make it try to drink the closest pond.

An implementation detail is how to pick all the sets of horizontal positions? We can use backtracking, or bit masks or itertools.combinations (in python)

Representing sets using bit masks is a trick used commonly during contests. If you are unfamiliar with this, you should give bmerry’s essential tutorial: A bit of fun: fun with bits a try.

Once the set is picked, we need to simulate the vertical and horizontal trunks. One way to do this is to treat the map as a grid. Make each trunk advance from its starting location to the first pond or point that is blocked. If the path is blocked, do nothing. Else make the elephant drink the pond and mark all the path as blocked. Then we need horizontal trunks to do the same. We can implement the logic for both columns and rows using the same algorithm.

import itertools

class ElephantDrinkingEasy:
  def maxElephants(self, theMap):
  n = len(theMap)
mx = 0
for r in xrange(2 * n + 1):
  for prows in itertools.combinations(xrange(2 * n), r):
  sim = [[theMap[i][j]
      for j in xrange(n)]
    for i in xrange(n)]
res = 0

# start a trunk in (x, y), advance by dx units in x and dy units in y:
  def simulate(x, y, dx, dy):
  while (0 <= x < n) and(0 <= y < n):
    if sim[x][y] == 'Y':
  sim[x][y] = '#'
return 1
elif sim[x][y] == '#':
  break
else:
  sim[x][y] = '#'
  (x, y) = (x + dx, y + dy)
return 0

for i in prows:
  x = i % n(y, dy) = (0, 1) if i < n
else(n - 1, -1)
res += simulate(x, y, 0, dy)
for j in xrange(2 * n):
  y = j % n(x, dx) = (0, 1) if j < n
else(n - 1, -1)
res += simulate(x, y, dx, 0)
mx = max(mx, res)
return mx

LCMSet

Problem Details

Used as: Division One - Level One:

Value250
Submission Rate552 / 647 (85.32%)
Success Rate316 / 552 (57.25%)
High ScoreWJMZBMR for 247.93 points (2 mins 36 secs)

Minimal LCM sets

One of the ideas in this problem is to notice that a set may have redundant elements. Elements that could have been created by taking the LCMs of the other elements. For example, a set like {2,3,9,6}, 6 is redundant because when we find the LCM Set, 6 would be created by taking the LCM of 2 and 3.

We can remove these redundant elements of a set without modifying the final “LCM Set”. Since the final LCM sets of A and B wouldn’t change after we “compress” them like this, then we can just solve the problem for the compressed sets and the equality wouldn’t change.

Note that each set will have a unique “compressed” set. If we remove one redundant element, it won’t affect our ability to remove another redundant element. We can prove this by contradiction: Assume redundant element a is one of the elements (a,b,c,…) such that their LCM is equal to another redundant element b, but since there should be other elements (x,y,z…) such that: LCM(x,y,z,…) = a is equal to a, then LCM(x,y,z,b,c,…) = LCM(a,b,c,…) - LCM is associative.

We have two sets A’ and B’ that have no redundant elements. We would like to know if their LCM sets are equal. A’ and B’ are both minimal, the only way for their final LCM sets to be equal is for A’ and B’ to be equal.

Compression

How can we remove the redundant elements of a set? We just iterate through each element x and if x can be made by taking the LCM of a subset of the other elements, then we don’t include it in the compressed set. Finding if x can be made by taking the LCM of a subset of a given set is exactly thedivision 2 version of this problem.

vector < int > compress(vector < int > S) {
  sort(S.begin(), S.end());
  vector < int > res(1, S[0]);
  for (int y: S) {
    /* include method from division 2 version of the problem */
    if (include(res, y) == "Impossible") {
      res.push_back(y);
    }
  }
  return res;
}
string equal(vector < int > A, vector < int > B) {
  return (compress(A) == compress(B)) ? "Equal" : "Not equal";
}

# "compress"

def compress(X):
  return {
    y
    for y in X
    if not div2solution([z
      for z in X
      if z != y], y)
  }

def div1solution(A, B):
  return compress(A) == compress(B)

Egalitarianism2

Problem Details

Used as: Division One - Level Two:

Value550
Submission Rate81 / 647 (12.52%)
Success Rate29 / 81 (35.80%)
High Scoreaceofdiamonds for 456.38 points (13 mins 27 secs)
Average Score287.15 (for 29 correct submissions)

We have a weighted graph of n nodes (cities), which is complete (all pairs of cities can be directly connected) and we would like to find a spanning tree with minimum standard deviation of edge weights.

If only we could predict the future

The trick in this problem is to think: What if the average weight of the best spanning tree was known? Let this average be m. The standard deviation is: sqrt( (sum( (w_i - m)^2 )) / (n-1) ) , (w_i are the weights of the picked edges and n is the number of cities). Minimizing this stddev requires us to minimize: sum( (w_i - m)^2 ) : For each edge in the tree we add (w_i - m)^2 to the cost. In fact, if we used a minimum spanning tree (MST) algorithm using (w_i - x)^2 as the edge cost, this would minimize the standard deviation.

… Wait, but what guarantee do we have that the average of the edges in the MST will be the same as m? In order to deal with this question we need some MST theory.

Ordering of edges and graph shape

If you consider Kruskal’s algorithm: The edges it picks for the MST depend on two things: The graph’s adjacency matrix (which vertices are connected with which others) and the ordering of the edges after we sort them by weight. Indeed, this is a property of the minimum spanning tree.

The topology of the graph will always be the same. What can change is the ordering of the edges by (w_i - m)^2, the value of m can modify this ordering. It will be helpful to analyze when exactly this happens. Two orderings A and B are different if there are two edges i and j such that (w_i - m)^2 < (w_j - m)^2 in ordering A and (w_i - m)^2 > (w_j - m)^2 in ordering B. Solve the inequality:

(w_i - m)^2 < (w_j - m)^2

w_i^2 -2mw_i + m^2 < w_j^2 -2mw_j + m^2

w_i^2 -2mw_i < w_j^2 -2mw_j

w_i^2 - w_j^2 < 2m( w_i - w_j )

( (w_i + w_j)(w_i - w_j) ) / ( w_i - w_j ) < m

( (w_i + w_j)(w_i - w_j) ) / ( w_i - w_j ) < 2m

(w_i + w_j) / 2 < m

If a case has (m >= (w_i + w_j) / 2) and another case has (m < (w_i + w_j) / 2), the orderings of the edges will be different. This depends solely on the value of m. In other words, for every pair of edges (i,j), there is a point (m = (w_i + w_j) / 2) at which the ordering changes.

Imagine we had a list of m_k such that: \exists i,j: m_k = (w_i + w_j)/2, Then we can claim that for each consecutive m_k, m_(k+1): The MST of edge weights (w_i-m)^2 for any m between m_k and m_(k+1), exclusive will be the same. So we can try to find the MST for any fixed m exclusively between m_k and m_(k+1) (m_k + \epsilon, where \epsilon is small enough to guarantee it m_k + \epsilon != m_(k+1) is a good enough candidate.) The resulting MST will have some edges, and we can calculate the new average and standard deviation of these edges. This standard deviation will be a candidate result.

A source of confusion about this approach is: What if the average edge weight is not equal to m_k + \epsilon? This doesn’t actually matter. We use m_k + \epsilon just to find an unique ordering of (w_i - m)^2 weights. This approach works by finding all Spanning Trees that could have the minimum standard deviation. Because it considers all the relevant cases of the average.

Another issue is: What to do with points that are equal to exactly one m_k ? In this case we will find that for the edges i and j: m - w_i = m - w_j, so either of them can come before the other. But trying m_(k-1) + \epsilon and m_k + \epsilon will already cover those two cases.

Complexity and code

For each pair of edges (i,j) there will be a value of m. There are O(n^2) edges, thus there are O(n^4) pairs of edges. Each pair requires us to solve the minimum spanning tree. In this case, the graph is a complete graph, which means that the simple adjacency-based implementation of Prim’s algorithm - which needs O(n^2) - will be a good choice. O(n^6) complexity in total.

// Euclidean distance:
double euclid(double x0, double y0, double x1, double y1) {
  double dx = x0 - x1;
  double dy = y0 - y1;
  return sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy);
}

int n;

// original edge weights:
double w[20][20];
// modified edge weights (m - w_ij)^2:
double wm[20][20];
double deviationPrim(double m) {
  // Do Prim's algorithm using (m - w[i][j]) ^ 2 as edge weights:
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
      wm[i][j] = (m - w[i][j]) * (m - w[i][j]);
    }
  }

  bool reach[n];
  fill(reach, reach + n, false);
  reach[0] = true;
  double dist[n];
  int parent[n];
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    dist[i] = wm[0][i];
    parent[i] = 0;
  }
  double r[n - 1];
  int t = 0;
  m = 0; // the actual average of the picked edges.
  for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
    pair < double, int > best = make_pair(1e100, -1);
    for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
      if (!reach[j]) {
        best = std::min(best, make_pair(dist[j], j));
      }
    }
    int j = best.second;
    assert(j != -1);
    reach[j] = true;
    r[t++] = w[parent[j]][j];
    m += r[t - 1] / (n - 1);

    for (int k = 0; k < n; k++) {
      if (dist[k] > wm[j][k]) {
        dist[k] = wm[j][k];
        parent[k] = j;
      }
    }
  }
  // calculate the standard deviation:
  double res2 = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
    res2 += (m - r[i]) * (m - r[i]) / (n - 1);
  }
  return sqrt(res2);
}

double minStdev(vector < int > x, vector < int > y) {
  n = x.size();
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
      w[i][j] = euclid(x[i], y[i], x[j], y[j]);
    }
  }
  double res = 1e9;

  // For each unordered pair of edges:
  for (int e1 = 0; e1 < n * n; e1++) {
    for (int e2 = e1 + 1; e2 < n * n; e2++) {
      // try (w_i + w_j) / 2
      double m = (w[e1 / n][e1 % n] + w[e2 / n][e2 % n]) / 2;
      res = std::min(res, deviationPrim(m + 1e-9));
    }
  }
  return res;
}

ElephantDrinking

Problem Details

Used as: Division One - Level Three:

Value1000
Submission Rate3 / 647 (0.46%)
Success Rate1 / 3 (33.33%)
High Scoretourist for 494.38 points (38 mins 15 secs)
Average Score494.38 (for 1 correct submission)

Maximums

Try to focus on the horizontal trunks only. Instead of deciding them all, think of the largest trunks that go from left to right and from right to left. Let’s call the maximum length of a L->R trunk a and the maximum of a R->L trunk b:

The important observation is to notice the difference between these two cases. In case 1 (The left), a+b <= n. This case is actually easy to solve. Just consider that if a, b are the maximum horizontal trunks as described, it divides the map in sections with different kinds of allowed trunk directions:

Because the vertical trunks cannot cross the maximum horizontal trunks, this limits the map in 5 sections. Each of these sections is an easy sub-problem to solve. If we picked the two larger trunks a and b and their positions, the remaining sections would be simple to solve. So we can try each possible a, b and their positions.

The issue is when a + b > n, a second case that is not as easy to solve. Here the approach is to also pick the maximum vertical c (from bottom to top) and d (from top to bottom). If c + d <= n we can just take it as a rotated version of case 1. So the only remaining case is when both a + b > n and c + d > n.

Once again, the map is divided in many sections each with different allowed directions. This time, there is even one section that does not allow any direction.

In a top level, the solution is just this, pick values and positions for the maximums and solve the created sub-problems. The sub-problems are each simple sub-problems, but there are many of them, so the problem needs a good approach to simplify the implementation time.

Implementation

In order to simplify implementation as much as possible, let’s start with case 2, which is quite complicated. A first challenge is that there are O(n^8) ways to pick the maximum a,b,c,d and their positions. A workaround is to notice that, once a,b,c,d are known, there are two ways to generate sections:

Thus we only need O(n^4) tuples (a,b,c,d) to consider all instances of case 2.

Another clever idea is to, consider each sub-problem as just the section of the map. If the rectangular section contains cells of the left-most column, then L->R trunks are allowed, if the section contains cells of the right-most column, then R->L trunks are allowed and so and so. This will group together the main problem in case 1, and many of the sub-problems as the same problem.

Most versions of this sub-problem can be solved using the same logic. For each allowed direction, pick the maximum trunk in that direction:

The trunk will separate the input section in sub-sections. This works almost every time, except in cases that contain only up/down or only left/right allowed directions. But we can just solve those cases that are 100% vertical or 100% horizontally following a simple logic (Pick the 1 or 2 best ponds in each row/column).

The complexity analysis: For each case in which at least 2 directions are allowed, the worst complexity is O(n^2). There are O(n^2) of those cases (remove at most two rows/columns/one row and one column). Each case in which there is only one allowed direction needs O(n). In total the complexity is O(n^4).

int dp[50][50][50][50];
int field[50][50];
int n;

int rec(int x0, int y0, int x1, int y1) {
  if (x0 > x1 || y0 > y1 || x0 < 0 || x1 >= n || y0 < 0 || y1 >= n) {
    return 0;
  }
  int & res = dp[x0][y0][x1][y1];
  if (res == -1) {

    bool U = (y0 == 0);
    bool D = (y1 == n - 1);
    bool R = (x0 == 0);
    bool L = (x1 == n - 1);

    int e, p, q, r;
    res = 0;

    if ((U || D) && !R && !L) {
      // special case: Only vertical moves:
      int s = U + D;
      // best s in column
      int a = 0, b = 0;
      for (int y = y0; y <= y1; y++) {
        int c = field[x1][y];
        if (c > a) {
          b = a;
          a = c;
        } else if (c > b) {
          b = c;
        }
      }
      res = a + ((s == 2) ? b : 0) + rec(x0, y0, x1 - 1, y1);
      return res;
    } else if (!U && !D && (R || L)) {
      // special case: Only horizontal moves:
      int s = R + L;
      int a = 0, b = 0;
      for (int x = x0; x <= x1; x++) {
        int c = field[x][y1];
        if (c > a) {
          b = a;
          a = c;
        } else if (c > b) {
          b = c;
        }
      }
      res = a + ((s == 2) ? b : 0) + rec(x0, y0, x1, y1 - 1);
      return res;
    }
    if (R) { //can go from right to left
      for (int y = y0; y <= y1; y++) {
        for (int x = 0; x <= x1; x++) {
          e = field[x][y];
          p = rec(0, y0, x, y - 1);
          q = rec(0, y + 1, x, y1);
          r = rec(x + 1, y0, x1, y1);
          res = std::max(res, e + p + q + r);
        }
      }
    }
    if (L) { //from left to right:
      for (int y = y0; y <= y1; y++) {
        for (int x = x0; x <= n - 1; x++) {
          e = field[x][y];
          p = rec(x, y0, n - 1, y - 1);
          q = rec(x, y + 1, n - 1, y1);
          r = rec(x0, y0, x - 1, y1);
          res = std::max(res, e + p + q + r);
        }
      }
    }
    if (U) { //can go from bottom to top
      for (int x = x0; x <= x1; x++) {
        for (int y = 0; y <= y1; y++) {
          e = field[x][y];
          p = rec(x0, 0, x - 1, y);
          q = rec(x + 1, 0, x1, y);
          r = rec(x0, y + 1, x1, y1);
          res = std::max(res, e + p + q + r);
        }
      }
    }
    if (D) { //can go from top to bottom
      for (int x = x0; x <= x1; x++) {
        for (int y = y0; y <= n - 1; y++) {
          e = field[x][y];
          p = rec(x0, y, x - 1, n - 1);
          q = rec(x + 1, y, x1, n - 1);
          r = rec(x0, y0, x1, y - 1);
          res = std::max(res, e + p + q + r);
        }
      }
    }
  }
  return res;
}

int maxSum(vector < string > field) {
  n = field.size();
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
      this - > field[i][j] = field[i][j] - '0';
    }
  }

  memset(dp, -1, sizeof(dp));
  int res = rec(0, 0, n - 1, n - 1); //Case 1

  // do case 2:
  for (int a = 1; a <= n; a++) {
    for (int b = n - a + 1; b <= n; b++) {
      for (int c = 1; c <= n; c++) {
        for (int d = n - c + 1; d <= n; d++) {
          int p, q, r, s;
          //2.1:
          p = rec(0, 0, n - b - 1, c - 1);
          q = rec(n - b, 0, n - 1, n - d - 1);
          r = rec(a, n - d, n - 1, n - 1);
          s = rec(0, c, a - 1, n - 1);
          res = std::max(res, p + q + r + s);

          //2.2
          p = rec(0, 0, a - 1, n - d - 1);
          q = rec(a, 0, n - 1, c - 1);
          r = rec(n - b, c, n - 1, n - 1);
          s = rec(0, n - d, n - b - 1, n - 1);
          res = std::max(res, p + q + r + s);
        }
      }
    }
  }

  return res;
}

Problem Details

Used as: Division Two - Level Two:

Divisors

Think of the very last move, unless smiles = 1, the last move must be a “paste” operation. This means that we must have reached some number x through some operations, copied it to the clipboard and pasted it some number of times to reach smiles. So x + x + … + x = “smiles”, therefore, x must be a divisor of “smiles”.

Let f(s) be a function that gives the minimum number of steps to have s smiles. Then f(“smiles”) is the solution to the overall problem. Let’s say we know which divisor x will be copied and pasted to reach s. Then we first need f(x) operations to reach x, 1 copy operation to put x in the clipboard and s/x - 1 operations (where s/x is integer division) to reach s. In short: f(x) + s/x. Of course, when s = 1, then the result is 0, because there is already one smile initially (base case).

Try them all

A good question is how to know what value of x to use? One approach is to just try all the possible values of x. Then we have the following recurrence relation:

  • If s=1, then the result is 0.

  • Else, find the minimum f(x) + s/x for every x that is a proper divisor of s (A divisor other than s).

The constraints are small enough that we can just implement the recurrence in this raw state, without memoization or dynamic programming. Like the following c++ and python solutions:

int printSmiles(int s) {
  int res = s; //if x = 1, then f(1) + s/1, f(1) = 0
  for (int x = 1; x < s; x++) {
    if (s % x == 0) { // x is a divisor of s
      res = std::min(res, printSmiles(x) + s / x);
    }
  }
  return res;
}
