Two rows

The grid is two rows of N nodes. A first observation we can make is that the only possible way to have 3 colinear points is if they are all in the same row. If we pick the path between two points in different rows, there will never be another point between them.

This is important because at any time, any move between any point in the current row and any unvisited point in the other point is possible.

Removing nodes

Let’s try a recursive-esque solution: After making a number of moves, count the number of ways to do the remaining moves. We should aim towards representing the state of the process. We need to keep track of two things: a) The current position and b) The points we already visited. We don’t really need to know the actual path before the previous position, it will not affect the number of ways to do the remaining moves.

In the image we have a current position and some points that were already visited. The arrows indicate the allowed moves from the current position. Here comes the realization that we can just consider visiting a node as the same as removing the node.

Current row

There are currently 4 things we need to know to represent the state: a) The current row. b) The horizontal position in the current row. c) The number of nodes in the top row. d) The number of nodes in the bottom row.

We can simplify things a bit by noticing that the case in which you are at the bottom row with x nodes in the top row and y nodes in the bottom row is exactly the same as the case in which you are at the top row with x nodes in the bottom row and x nodes in the top row. In other words we will now define the state by only 3 variables: c the horizontal position in the current row. x the number of nodes in the current row and y: the number of nodes in the other row.

Always alternating rows

At the moment the number of states is O(n^3) and in each state there are O(n) other states to visit ( 2 of the available moves can change the horizontal position, but O(n) moves take you to any of the nodes in the other row).

It would be nice, for example if we could reduce the state space to O(n^2). The horizontal position c is only important to determine which horizontal moves are allowed. Imagine we decided to stop making horizontal moves, then no matter what the position is, we can pick any new cell in the other row. Then we will do some horizontal moves, until it is time to change rows again, and once again, we can pick any cell in the other row.

Making any number of consecutive horizontal moves will always remove a group of contiguous nodes. What we can do is pick the segment of contiguous horizontal nodes we want to remove, count the number of ways to do it and then change row. A function f(x,y) consists of this. The current row has x nodes, and we are supposed to pick a group of contiguous horizontal nodes in this row and remove them. After that, we can move to the other row. We can pick any node as the first node to visit in this row. Note that picking any contiguous group of i nodes will yield the same result. So for each i, we calculate the number of ways to remove exactly i nodes from the current row and move to the other row:

  • There are (x - i + 1) starting positions for the contiguous group of i nodes. We can pick any of them.

  • The number of distinct ways to remove that group depends on i , for now we will call it g(i).

  • After that, we can move to the other row, once again we can pick any starting node in that row and remove a group of contiguous nodes. The current row changes and the and the former current row has i less nodes: f(y, x - i)

    f(x,y) = sum_(i = 1)^(i <= x) (x - i + 1) g(i) f(y, x- i)

A base case: If x = 0, then it means there are no remaining cells in the current row. We cannot make any more moves. If y = 0, then we have visited all cells and the result is 1 (one valid way to finish). Else the result is 0 (it is impossible to finish).

Assuming we memoize the results and we precalculate g(i) this formula allows us to solve the problem in O(n^3) time. Still not enough.

Number of ways to remove n horizontal nodes

Before we try to optimize that formula, we should understand the g(n) function. It counts the number of ways to do only horizontal moves until you remove n contiguous points. So this is basically the same as solving the original problem but for only one row of n nodes.

The first thing is to pick an initial node and call it the position. So we define a new function h(n, i) counts the number of ways to visit all the nodes in a row of n nodes if the initial position is i. So g(n) = sum_(i=0)^(i<n)h(n,i).

Solving h(n,i) is easy if you keep in mind that to keep track of visited points we can just remove them:

  • If n = 1, then there is only one point and we already visited it. Base case returns 1, one valid way to do it.

  • If i > 0, then we can move left. The position decreases, but we should also remove the point i that was already visited. h(n - 1, i - 1).

  • If i + 1 < n, then we can move right. The position is supposed to increase, but in reality, the previous point is removed, which decrements n and in fact, also moves the position left again, so i stays the same: h(n - 1,i).

  • The sum of those two cases yields h(n,i)

This yields an O(n^2) solution if we use memoization. But what we really need is optimize the O(n^3) time calculation of f(). We need to know more about g() and h().

One method is to just code the O(n^2) solution and then print the results. You will notice that g(i) is always a power of 2. g(i) = 2^(i-1). How could we reach this fact formally?

The trick is to notice that h(i,n) is usually a sum: h(n-1,i-1) + h(n-1,i). This is reminiscent of the Pascal triangle and suggests us that the result is a Binomial coefficient. g(n) is defined as a sum of Binomial coefficients, which also tends to be equal to a power of 2…

Quadratic

Our formula now looks like this:

f(x,y) = sum_(i = 1)^(i <= x) (x - i + 1) 2 ^ (i - 1) f(y, x- i)

How does the power of 2 help us? One idea is to notice that if you have a sum of 2^i, you can multiply this sum by 2 and you have a sum of 2^(i+1), or you can divide by 2 and you have a sum of 2^(i-1). The power of 2 might help us move from i to i+1 easily. What we should try is find a relationship between f(x,y) and f(x-1,y).

The formula for f(x-1,y) looks like this:

f(x-1,y) = sum_(i = 1)^(i <= x-1) (x-1 - i + 1) 2 ^ (i - 1) f(y, x-1- i)

We need to modify f(x,y) in a way to make that appear… We want things like x - i to turn into x-i-1. If we change i into i+1, it would be great. An idea would be to change the indexing from going from i = 1 to i <= x into going from i + 1 <= n.

f(x,y) = sum_(i+1 = 1)^(i+1 <= x) (x - i - 1 + 1) 2 ^ (i + 1 - 1) f(y, x - i - 1) f(x,y) = sum_(i = 0)^(i <= x-1) (x - 1 - i + 1) 2 ^ (i) f(y, x - 1 - i)

There are two differences from this and the formula of f(x-1,y), one is that this sum starts from 0. What we can do is take the result for i=0 out of the sum. Like this:

f(x,y) = (x - 1 - 0 + 1) 2 ^ (0) f(y, x - 1 - 0) + sum_(i = 1)^(i <= x-1) (x - 1 - i + 1) 2 ^ (i) f(y, x - 1 - i)

For the 2^i part, imagine each member in the sum has an extra 2 factor, so we take that factor out of the sum:

f(x,y) = (x - 1 - 0 + 1) 2 ^ (0) f(y, x - 1 - 0) + 2sum_(i = 1)^(i <= x-1) (x - 1 - i + 1) 2 ^ (i-1) f(y, x - 1 - i) f(x,y) = xf(y, x - 1) + 2sum_(i = 1)^(i <= x-1) (x - 1 - i + 1) 2 ^ (i-1) f(y, x - 1 - i)

The sum part is now exactly the formula for f(x-1,y). There is a small detail here. We have a base case for f(0,y), so the definition of f(x-1,y) is only that summation if x - 1 != 0, so replacing this summation with f(x-1,y) is only valid when x > 1. The x = 1 case can be solved separately or noticing that it is exactly equal to just xf(y,x-1), because there was only one member in the sum. This appears unimportant , but if we don’t notice the issue, the result of using the formula will yield 3x times the correct result, and in this problem, division is made complicated because the MOD is not a constant and not necessarily prime.

For x > 1 the formula is correct:

f(x,y) = xf(y, x - 1) + 2f(x - 1, y)

This is very nice because it is a recurrence with O(n^2) states and each state requires O(1)` calculations. The resulting code is deceivingly simple, it really hides all the analysis that we needed for it:

Problems that ask you to return the result modulo 1,000,000,007 (or a similar number) usually do so to allow us to solve problems that have large results without actually using those large numbers in calculations. We can handle these numbers with modular arithmetic. Read this recipe for more info.

int MOD;

int dp[3501][3501];

long f(int x, int y) {
  long res = dp[x][y];
  if (res == -1) {
    if (x == 0) {
      // done?
      if (y == 0) {
        res = 1;
      } else {
        res = 0;
      }
    } else {
      // Time limit is very tight. Beware of using % too many times.
      // Maybe implementing this iteratively would be better in other languages
      long a = (x * f(y, x - 1)) % MOD;
      long b = ((x > 1) ? ((2 * f(x - 1, y))) : 0); //only when x > 1
      res = (a + b) % MOD;
    }
    res = dp[x][y] = (int) res;
  }
  return res;
}

int solve(int N, int MOD) {
  this - > MOD = MOD;
  memset(dp, -1, sizeof(dp));
  // we can pick any of the two rows as the initial "current" row:
  return (2 * f(N, N)) % MOD;
}

The problem asks us to find if t is a subsequence of s. So we need to return a string in case it is possible to remove zero or more characters from s to convert it into t.

This is a classical problem. The constraints are also so low that we could even brute force for the subset of characters to remove from s and check if the remmant is equal to t. There are O(|s| + |t|) approaches, however:

Imagine the first character in t, t[0], this character must exist in s, if it doesn’t , then t is not a subsequence. If it does, then we still need to look for the other characters of t, however, we need to make sure that we find t[1] in a position after the position of t[0], then t[2] and so and so.

The following c++ approach is based on matching. Use a pointer j that initially is zero (points to the start of t). Visit the characters in s from left to right with index i. Whenever s[i] = t[j], increment j because this means we have found that the wanted letter in t exists and is in the correct order, so we should now look for the next one. If we find all characters in t, it is a subsequence.

string check(string s, string t) {
  int j = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < s.size() && j < t.size(); i++) {
    if (s[i] == t[j]) {
      j++;
    }
  }
  return (j == t.size()) ? "Yep, it's wood." : "Nope.";
}

We can also use a recursive logic. Like in this python solution:

def SubSequence(s, t):
  if t == "": # empty string is always a subsequence
return True
if s == "": # t is non empty and s is empty, impossible
return False
if s[0] == t[0]:
  return SubSequence(s[1: ], t[1: ]) #delete both starting characters
else:
  return SubSequence(s[1: ], t) #delete only the first character of s

class IdentifyingWood:
  def check(self, s, t):
  return "Yep, it's wood."
if SubSequence(s, t)
else "Nope."

If the teacher is late x time units, the delay in the starting time decreases x time units from the class duration. In addition, the penalty for the ending time decreases other x^2 units. We need to find the maximum d such that:

max d: d - x - x^2 >= 0

Brute-force

Even just a brute-force search can work in time assuming we avoid overhead. A way to minimize the overhead is: Start with x = 0, and increment x until d - x - x*x < 0, the final value of x will be the smallest wrong one. Subtract one and you’ll find the largest correct one:

1
2
3
4
5
6
7
8
9
long getTime(long d) {
  long x = 1;

  while (d - x - x * x >= 0) {
    x++;
  }

  return x - 1;
}

Why is this fast enough? It all depends on the largest possible result. This is accomplished with the largest possible input d = 10^18. d - x - x*x >= 0, which means that if x was larger than sqrt(d) , it would be invalid. So the approach is O(sqrt(d)). So we can guess that the number of steps will be less than 10^9. The operations are simple (addition, product, comparison) and it turns out that topcoder servers can run 10^9 simple operations under the time limit.

Binary search

If we didn’t want to take risks and want to have a much safer time complexity, there are other approaches.

The binary search solution uses the same knowledge as the previous algorithm. We know that the set of valid x will be an interval from 0 to the result, inclusive. d >= x + x*x is all about x being too large. This means that if a Y (small enough) is a valid result for x, then any 0 <= Z <= Y is also valid. In addition, if Y is invalid (too large) then any Z > Y will be even larger hence also invalid.

def BinarySearch(lo, hi, f):
  # f(lo) is true
# f(hi) is false
# returns the largest x such that f(x) is true
while lo + 1 < hi:
  ha = (lo + hi) / 2
if f(ha):
  lo = ha
else:
  hi = ha
return lo

class QuadraticLaw:
  def getTime(self, d):
  return BinarySearch(0, d + 1, lambda x: d - x - x * x >= 0)

This approach will be O(log(d)).

Quadratic formula

max d: d - x - x^2 >= 0

We solve the quadratic equation:

d - x - x^2 = 0 x^2 + x - d = 0 A x^2 + Bx + C = 0 | A = 1, B = 1, C = -d

We can use the Quadratic formula:

x = ( -B +-sqrt(B^2 - 4AC ) ) / (2A) x = ( -1 +-sqrt(1 + 4d) ) / 2

There are two solutions to +- sqrt(1 + 4d), but we should only consider the positive one, else the whole result of x will be negative (adding -1 then dividing by 2.

x_1 = ( sqrt(1 + 4d) - 1) / 2

As long as x <= x_1, then d - x - x^2 <= 0, hence we need to find the largest integer that is less than or equal to x_1. This is the description of the floor function.

1
2
3
long getTime(long d) {
  return floorl((sqrtl(4 * d + 1) - 1) / 2);
}

This requires extra care because we are using float operations but the result is expected to be an integer. The sqrt function is not a stable operation, which will increase the error. In this case, it turns out that using the long versions of sqrt and floor (sqrtl and floorl) is enough to pass the system tests.

We are allowed to remove an edge and then add a new edge of the same length. A tree will have up to 1999 edges (N - 1). An approach we can try is try each of the edges and find the best solution if that edge is removed. When finding the best solution for each edge, we need to be careful about complexity. O(N) should be the aim.

Two trees

After an edge is removed we will have two trees. After adding the new edge, the result must be a tree again. This means that the new edge must connect the two trees.

The objective is to have the maximum possible diameter.

The main idea is to make it so the diameter of the final tree is a path that travels through both trees. If we want to make this as large as possible, we need to pick one path from each of the trees and then include an edge that connects the two paths. If these two paths were the largest possible - the diameters of the two trees. Then the final path connecting them both will be the largest possible diameter too.

Calculating the diameter

We describe the best possible result after removing a specific edge as the sum of the diameters of the two trees created after removing the edge plus the length of the edge. The problem is to just find the diameter of the two trees. Remember that we should aim to O(N) time. This is actually a classic problem.

Code

Time constraints are tight, the algorithm is O(N^2), but we need to avoid the constant factor. We need to do 4 BFS / DFS operations to find all distances to a node in tree, for each of the O(N) edges. For example, we can avoid to re-create the whole tree data structure for each edge we try to remove.

const long INF = 4000000000000 LL;
int n;
// Storing the graph. Each g[i] is a list of edges adjacent to vertex i
// each edge is a pair (j,e), where j is the other vertex connected to i
// and e is the id of the edge. So L[e] is the weight of the edge.
vector < list < tuple < int, int >>> g;
vector < int > L;

long dist[2000];

// Calculate the distance from x to all other vertices in the tree, assumes
// edge # ignoredEdge has been deleted.
void bfs(int x, int ignoredEdge) {
  fill(dist, dist + n, INF);
  queue < int > Q;
  dist[x] = 0;
  Q.push(x);

  while (!Q.empty()) {
    int x = Q.front();
    Q.pop();
    for (auto p: g[x]) {
      int y, e;
      tie(y, e) = p;
      if (e != ignoredEdge) {
        long d = dist[x] + L[e];
        if (d < dist[y]) {
          dist[y] = d;
          Q.push(y);
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

// calculate the diameter of tree containing vertex x, ignores vertices
// not connected to it. Assumes edge # ignoredEdge has been removed
long diameter(int x, int ignoredEdge) {
  // find the diameter of the tree that contains vertex x

  // A BFS from x
  bfs(x, ignoredEdge);
  // find the furthest y from x:
  int y = -1;
  for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
    if ((dist[j] < INF) && ((y == -1) || (dist[y] < dist[j]))) {
      y = j;
    }
  }

  // A BFS from y:
  bfs(y, ignoredEdge);
  long dia = 0;
  for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
    if (dist[j] < INF) {
      dia = max(dia, dist[j]);
    }
  }
  return dia;
}

long getLength(vector < int > A, vector < int > B, vector < int > L) {
  int n = A.size() + 1;
  this - > L = L;
  this - > n = n;
  g.resize(n);
  for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
    g[A[i]].push_back(make_tuple(B[i], i));
    g[B[i]].push_back(make_tuple(A[i], i));
  }
  long res = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
    // We know A[i] is in one of the trees and B[i] in the other.
    res = std::max(res, diameter(A[i], i) + diameter(B[i], i) + L[i]);
  }
  return res;
}

Understanding the statement

This is a problem in which it is important to understand what we need to find.

An interval [a,b) is just a sequence of indexes a,a+1, …, b - 1 in the rating graph.

An interval [i, i + t] is a scaled version of another [j, j + t] (note that the lengths t of the intervals must be equal). If:

  • i != j (So they are not exactly the same graph)

  • There exists a factor f such that: For every k: The two segments (i + k, i + k + 1) and (j + k, j + k + 1) are scaled to that factor. This means that every x[j+k +1] - x[j+k] = f*( x[i+k +1] - x[i+k]) and y[j+k +1] - y[j+k] = f*( y[i+k +1] - y[i+k])

  • The total length of the curves in one of the intervals is as large as possible.

(We are using x[] to represent the times and y[] to represent the ratings).

Checking the intervals

If we tried every possible combination of (i,j,t) and then checked in O(t) time if all the segments were scaled up to the same factor, we would have an O(N^4) solution, too slow. We can take it to O(N^3) with this simple trick: We try every pair of the beginning index for the two intervals: (i,j), we can check if segments (i, i+1) and (j, j+1) are scaled. If so we can find f and then repeat for k= 2, 3, … and check the segments (i+k-1, i+k) and (j+k-1, j+k) in each step until we find an invalid pair. This will be O(n^3). The rest is implementation details.

Checking the first segment.

For (i,j) the first segments of the intervals should be equivalent:

x[j+1] - x[j] = f*( x[i+1] - x[i]) y[j+1] - y[j] = f*( y[i+1] - y[i])

For convenience:

d_(x_2) = f*d_(x_1) d_(y_2) = f*d_(y_1)

We need to check that the factor is equal in both dimensions:

d_(x_2) / d_(x_1) = d_(y_2) / d_(y_1)

This equation is fine, but the result is boolean and division would bring floating point values. It is best to avoid floating point operations when the result is discrete. Else we would need to worry about possible errors (a division could return 5.000000001 and the other 5.00000000199 because of precision issues even though the actual values are real). Since all the values are initially integers, we can avoid this by moving the divisors:

d_(x_2) * d_(y_1) = d_(y_2) * d_(x_1)

If these two values are equal, the first segments of the intervals are equivalent. We just need to check that all the other segments are scaled to the same factor, to this we need to find f:

f = d_(x_2) / d_(x_1) = d_(y_2) / d_(y_1)

There are a couple of things to be careful about. First of all, d_(y_1) can be 0 (no rating change) but d_(x_1) cannot (all dates are distinct). So we should use the x equation to calculate f. Second, once again we find a division and thus floating points. I recommend doing this instead:

p = d_(x_2) q = d_(x_1) f = p / q

We will represent f as a fraction p / q.

Finally for each k = 2, 3,... we need to check that segments (i+k-1,i+k) and (j+k-1,j+k) are correctly scaled up. This means that we need to verify:

x[j+k] - x[j+k-1] = (p / q) * (x[i+k] - x[i+k-1])

We can get rid of the division:

q * (x[j+k] - x[j+k-1]) = p * (x[i+k] - x[i+k-1])

And a similar situation for y.

The rest is, once we find the maximum k for which it is correct, calculate the sum of the lengths of each segment in both intervals and remember the maximum we can find.

double maxLengthSlow(vector < int > date, vector < int > rating) //0.7 s
{
  int n = date.size();
  double res = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i + 1 < n; i++) {
    for (int j = 0; j + 1 < n; j++) {
      if (i != j) {
        long dx1 = date[i + 1] - date[i];
        long dy1 = rating[i + 1] - rating[i];
        long dx2 = date[j + 1] - date[j];
        long dy2 = rating[j + 1] - rating[j];

        // dy1 / dx1 = dy2 / dx2
        if (dy1 * dx2 == dy2 * dx1) {
          double sum = sqrtl(dx1 * dx1 + dy1 * dy1);

          long factorP = date[j + 1] - date[j];
          long factorQ = date[i + 1] - date[i];
          // the remaining segments in j are all equal to the ones in
          // i but scaled up by P/Q 
          for (int k = 2; i + k < n && j + k < n; k++) {
            dx1 = date[i + k] - date[i + k - 1];
            dy1 = rating[i + k] - rating[i + k - 1];
            dx2 = date[j + k] - date[j + k - 1];
            dy2 = rating[j + k] - rating[j + k - 1];

            // dx2 == (dx1) * (P/Q)
            if ((dx2 * factorQ == dx1 * factorP) &&
              (dy2 * factorQ == dy1 * factorP)
            ) {
              sum += sqrtl(dx1 * dx1 + dy1 * dy1); //add the Euclidean distance
            } else {
              break;
            }
          }
          res = std::max(res, sum);
        }
      }
    }
  }
  return res;
}

Problem Details

Used as: Division One - Level Two:

If we knew what rounds to change

Imagine we decided to pick a subset of rounds to change. For this subset only we would have a problem: There are t cutoffs and t places and we should rearrange the places so that each “place”[i] <= “cutoff”[i].

Consider the smallest place p among the places in the chosen subset. If the smallest cutoff c is larger than this place, then no matter what we do, it will be impossible to arrange the places in a correct way. If p <= c, then it is possible to match the smallest place with the smallest cut-off. After doing this match, there will be some remaining places and cutoffs, it will be best to match the second-smallest place with the second-smallest cutoff and so and so.

As a proof, try induction. This strategy is true for t = 1 (It is the only strategy). Now we just need to prove that if it is true for t = k - 1, then it is also true for t = k. Imagine the sorted list of places: p_0 <= p_1 <= … <= p_(k-1) and the sorted list of cutoffs: c_0 <= c_1 <= … <= c_(k-1) . If p_0 <= c_0 then we need to show that matching c_0 with p_0 is the optimal decision. Because the strategy is correct for t = k-1, this match will mean that p_1 and c_1 are matched, then p_2 and c_2 and so and so. Compare to a different move: Matching p_0 with c_1, for example. Because of the strategy being true for t = k - 1, this means that the remaining matches will include: p_1 with c_0. Because p_0 <= p_1, the constraint p_1 <= c_0 will usually be tighter than p_1 <= c_0 (and thus this decision will have a greater possibility to fail), unless p_0 = p_1, in which case both moves are exactly the same. We can find that the effect is similar when matching p_0 with any c_i : i != 0.

The rounds that must be changed

There are some rounds in which “cutoff”[i] < “place”[i] and those rounds are already invalid unless we include them in the set. This set might already be the solution, we just need to test if that greedy algorithm above fails or not.

Imagine the greedy algorithm fails. It means that after we sorted the bad set’s places and cutoffs: c_0 <= c_1 <= … and p_0 <= p_1 <= … . There is at least one i such that the cutoff is too small: p_i > c_i. Consider the smallest index i for which: p_i > c_i. The problem is that the place is too large for that cutoff. There is no alternative but to add at least one new round to the set. There are currently some “good” rounds j for which “cutoff”[j] >= “place”[j]. We need to pick one of them and add it to the set. Which one?

The main issue is that c_i is too small of a cutoff and there is currently not a single “place” that we can assign to this cut-off. So a important requirement is that we add a round j that has “place”[j] <= c_i. If no such a round exists, there is no solution.

If many rounds with “place”[j] <= c_i exist, then we need to pick one of them. Note that “place”[j] will be matched with c_i, so the relationship between “place”[j] and “cutoff”[j] is unimportant. So our focus is picking the best possible new “cutoff”[j]. Intuitively, largest is best. This will only increase the possibility that any next “place” will have a space available. Note that after adding this new place, the remaining places and cutoffs might need to be sorted again. (Or we should save them in an ordered structure). We already know that the first i rounds in the set match correctly, but we need to check for index i again (the i-th round/place changed).

Complexity analysis: We need an O(n) search for every i index that fails. There will be at most O(n) times this happen. If you re-sort the places/cutoffs that adds a O(n log(n)) factor. In total: O(n^2 log(n)) which is okay for n <= 1000.

int minimumChanges(vector < int > places, vector < int > cutoff) {
  vector < int > goodplace, goodcutoff;
  vector < bool > goodadded;

  vector < int > badplace, badcutoff;

  for (int i = 0; i < places.size(); i++) {
    if (places[i] <= cutoff[i]) {
      goodplace.push_back(places[i]);
      goodcutoff.push_back(cutoff[i]);
      goodadded.push_back(false);
    } else {
      badplace.push_back(places[i]);
      badcutoff.push_back(cutoff[i]);
    }
  }
  sort(badplace.begin(), badplace.end());
  sort(badcutoff.begin(), badcutoff.end());

  int i = 0;
  while (i < badplace.size()) {
    if (badplace[i] > badcutoff[i]) {
      // we need to add more
      // the added place must be >= badplace[i]
      //  - This also guarantees the added cutoff is >= badplace[i]
      // the added cutoff should be as large as possible
      int r = -1;
      for (int j = 0; j < goodplace.size(); j++) {
        if (!goodadded[j] &
          amp; & amp;
          (goodplace[j] <= badcutoff[i]) &
          amp; & amp;
          ((r == -1) || (goodcutoff[r] < goodcutoff[j]))
        ) {
          r = j;
        }
      }
      if (r == -1) {
        return -1;
      } else {
        // update, leave i unchanged
        goodadded[r] = true;
        badplace.push_back(goodplace[r]);
        badcutoff.push_back(goodcutoff[r]);
        sort(badplace.begin() + i, badplace.end());
        sort(badcutoff.begin() + i, badcutoff.end());
      }
    } else {
      // move to next i
      i++;
    }
  }
  return badplace.size();
}

ColourHolic

Problem Details

Used as: Division One - Level Three:

Value1000
Submission Rate11 / 602 (1.83%)
Success Rate7 / 11 (63.64%)
High Scoretourist for 757.39 points (17 mins 16 secs)
Average Score578.93 (for 7 correct submissions)

I’m also the author of SRM 635 - so this problem ends up having an author’s editorial! The hero of the story isn’t based on me, however, so I’ll stick to more common four colours: R G B and yellow ( Y).

We want to find the number of arrays that use each of the 4 colours a given number of times, with an additional condition that no two adjacent colours in one array may be equal. Let’s take an example array.

The trick used here is some sort of meet-in-the-middle approach. Suppose that we split the colours into 2 pairs (for example B- R, G- Y) and cut the array between colours that don’t belong to the same pairs.

We get a sequence of segments, each of which only uses 2 colours. But if we only have 2 colours in some segment, then they must alternate (the exact order is given by the first colour and length of the segment), and the pairs of colours used in individual segments must also alternate, because of the way we created them. These two conditions are also sufficient for keeping adjacent colours distinct, so we’ve reformulated the problem as “find the number of ways to form a sequence of segments using each of the 4 colours a given number of times”.

Now’s the right time to decide on a smart way to asssociate an array with the two pairs of colours. The first two colours of any array must be distinct, so choose them as one pair p_1 and the other two as the other pair p_2. We can try all pairs p_1 and count the number of sequences of segments that start with p_1, i.e. the first segment has length ≥ 2 and uses p_1.

Suppose that we knew how to count the number of sequences where the first segment uses p_1; denote that by count_4(p_1,n[]). From that, we need to subtract cases where the first segment has length 1. That means the first segment is formed by just one colour c from the pair p_1 and after it follows a sequence where the first segment uses p_2, so we can just subtract the number of such sequences for each choice of c.


solve(n[]):
answer =0
for all p1 = (i,j); 0 ≤ i < j < 4
p2 = (the other two colours)
answer += count4(p1,n[])
n[i] -=1
answer -= count4(p2,n[])
n[i] +=1; n[j] -=1
answer -= count4(p2,n[])
n[j] +=1
return answer

Moving on to computing count_4(p_1,n[]): we can try all possible choices for s - the number of segments that use p_2. Since the pairs used in segments alternate, the number of segments that use p_1 must be either s (if the last segment uses pair 2) or s+1 (if it uses pair 1). For each case, we’ve now split the problem into two subproblems, one for each pair: “find the number of ways to form a sequence of s segments that use the same pair of colours p, using each of these 2 colours a given number of times”. Denote the answer to that by count_2(p,s,n[]) and the maximum of n[] by N.


count4(p1,n[]):
ret =0
for all 0 ≤ s ≤ N
ret += count2(p2,s,n[]) * ( count2(p1,s,n[]) + count2(p1,s+1,n[]) )
return ret

We want to compute count_2(p,s,n[]). Suppose that the 2 colours in p ( R and B, for example) need to be used r and b times, respectively. Let’s take a look at computing the number of times each colour is used in one segment. It differs for segments of even and odd length. For segments of even length 2k, we can see that each colour is used k times, regardless of the first colour in that segment. For odd lengths 2k+1, the first colour in it is used k+1 times and the other one k times. To account for this difference, let’s try fixing the number o of odd segments; that means there are e=s-o even segments.

If we know o, we can find out how many (o_r) odd segments start with R and how many (o_b) with B. Clearly, o_r+o_b=o. Since starting a segment with some colour means using it one more time than the other one, R will be used o_r-o_b times more than B; however, we know that R must be used r-b times more than B, so o_r-o_b=r-b. Solving this system of linear equations, we find out that o_a=( o + r-b )/2 and o_b=( o + b-r )/2.

The next thing to do would be the actual assignment of parities to segments. We’re just trying to pick e even segments in a sequence of s; there are C(s,o) ways to do that. Then, let’s assign the first colour to each segment. For even segments, both choices ( R and B) are equally possible, so there are 2^e ways. For odd ones, we know that exactly o_r must start with R and the rest with B; there are again C(o,o_r) ways to pick o_r out of o odd segments.

All that’s left is choosing the lengths of segments; in order to avoid making segments of length 0 later, we’ll consider the first colour of each odd segment and the first 2 colours ( R and B) of each even segment to be used in them already. Choosing the length of a segment to be 2l+2 or 2l+1 (depending on its parity; l \ge 0) means using each colour ( R and B) l times in it; we can view this as using l pairs p in that segment. The total number of pairs we need to use in all segments this way has to be r-o_r=b-o_b=\frac{r+b-o}{2}-e=k.

We just got a subproblem “find the number of ways to split k identical objects in s distinct bins”. That’s a well known problem (you may read about it on top of page 3 here); the answer to it is C(s+k-1,k-1).

To compute count_2(p,s,n[]), we just need to sum up the number of ways for all o between 0 and s (inclusive, to account for some colour being used 0 times). Problem solved - to sum it up (omitting modulos):


count2(p,s,n[]):
// p == (i,j)
r = n[i], b = n[j]
ret =0
for all 0 ≤ o ≤ s
e = s-o
o_r = (o+r-b)/2
k = (r+b-o)/2-e
if o_r integer: ret += C(s,o) * C(o,o_r) * 2**e * C(s+k-1,k-1)
return ret

Hold on, this approach seems to take O(N^2) time! Well, as it’s written here, yes - but a few obvious conditions are all that’s needed to make it work in time. These are:

  • for C(o,o_r) to be non-zero, 0 ≤ o_r ≤ o, which can be simplified to |r-b| ≤ o

  • there’s no way to make s non-empty segments if r+b < s, so s ≤ r+b

Since o ≤ s, we get r-b ≤ o ≤ r+b and b-r ≤ o ≤ b+r, which means there are just O(min(r,b)) choices for o and O(r+b) choices for s. The latter limits the number of choices for s to around 400 (instead of N) in case the 2 colours that are used at most 200 times are in the same pair, the former limits the number of choices for o to around 400 if they’re in different pairs. With these conditions, the time complexity is something like O(400N), which is good enough to pass.

We’ve been using binomial coefficients modulo M all the time. An explanation on computing them is given here. If we precalculate factorials and their modular inverses in O(N\log M) time, each binomial coefficient can be computed in O(1) time.

This problem has two parts, the first is to know how to find the feeding times given the input. The two pets work under the same logic with 3 integers: s, p and t. The times make arithmetic sequences.

A way to find the times. Start with x = s, this is the first time. Repeat t-1 steps: x = x + p, the new x becomes a time.

The second part of the problem is to find if there is at least one time that is repeated in both sequences. To do this, we should first save each of the pets’ feeding times in two lists. The maximum t is 1000, so we can actually just use an O(t_1 t_2) loop for all pairs of times, if we find a time that appears in both sequences, the result is “Difficult”.

list < int > getSeq(int st, int p, int t) {
  list < int > res;
  int x = st;
  for (int i = 0; i < t; i++) {
    res.push_back(x);
    x += p;
  }
  return res;
}

string isDifficult(int st1, int p1, int t1, int st2, int p2, int t2) {
  list < int > a = getSeq(st1, p1, t1);
  list < int > b = getSeq(st2, p2, t2);

  for (int x: a) {
    for (int y: b) {
      if (x == y) {
        return "Difficult";
      }
    }
  }

  return "Easy";
}

Python taking advantage of the range() library function and this time using set instead of lists, because sets allow finding the intersection of two sets quickly (If the intersection is non-empty, there is a time that is included in both sequences):

class ElectronicPetEasy:
  def isDifficult(self, st1, p1, t1, st2, p2, t2):
  a = set(range(st1, st1 + t1 * p1, p1))
b = set(range(st2, st2 + t2 * p2, p2))
return "Difficult"
if len(a & b)
else "Easy"

string isDifficult(int st1, int p1, int t1, int st2, int p2, int t2) {
  for (int i = st1; i < st1 + t1 * p1; i += p1) {
    for (int j = st2; j < st2 + t2 * p2; j += p2) {
      if (i == j) {
        return "Difficult";
      }
    }
  }
  return "Easy";
}

AliceGameEasy

Problem Details

Used as: Division Two - Level Two:

Value500
Submission Rate487 / 763 (63.83%)
Success Rate331 / 487 (67.97%)
High ScoreMiraculousCoder for 496.68 points (2 mins 19 secs)
Average Score320.81 (for 331 correct submissions)

The number of turns

In each turn i, one of the players earns i points. If we are given both Alice’s and Kirito’s final scores, we can combine them to a single total t. t must be the sum of all i from 1 to the number of turns n. Meaning that we can find the number of turns by looking for an n such that (n(n+1)) / 2 = t (This is the summation formula). Although due to the constraints, t is at most 10^12, we can use simple search to find a valid value of n by incrementing it from 1. (Once n(n+1)/2 becomes larger than t, we can stop looking and declare the case invalid). n(n+1)/2 grows up quadratically, so this search will be O(sqrt(t)).

Possible for Alice

Once we know n, we also know that a search from 1 to n is O(sqrt(t)), we can actually just try all values a for the number of turns Alice won and answer “is it possible for Alice to win a turns?”. If the answer is “no” for all numbers from 0 to n, then the case is invalid. Else we just need to return the smallest valid a.

How to know if it is possible for Alice to win a turns? In this case, one way to answer this is first analyze what is the smallest score Alice can get by winning only a turns: l = 1 + 2 + 3 + … + a. It is also possible to know what is the maximum possible score: h = n + (n-1) + (n-2) + … (n-a+1). So we know that if x (Alice’s score) was smaller than l or higher than h then this number of won Alice games is not possible. As a necessary condition: l <= x <= h.

The trick is to notice that l <= x <= h is also a sufficient condition. In other words, Alice can score any total score x between l and h by winning exactly a games. This is easier to understand with an example: Imagine a was 3 and n was 10. The minimum is: 1+2+3 = 6 and the maximum is: 10+9+8 = 27. Take the minimum, we can replace 3 with 4 or any number up to 10, this means that 7, 8, … 13 are possible. Or we can take the maximum 10+9+8 and replace the 8 with any smaller number, this means 26, 25, 24, …, 20 would be possible too. By replacing some numbers with others we are able to reach any x within the bounds.

A formal proof: Let l and h be the lower and upper bounds for the total score winning a turns. We can prove that if l and h are possible, then l+1 is also possible. l is the sum of a numbers: l_1, l_2, …, l_a. This cannot be the maximum possible sequence of numbers. Because if it were, then a valid value of h that is at least 1 unit larger than l wouldn’t exist. What we need to visualize here is that there were n numbers, we picked a numbers but we didn’t pick the exact sequence of the a largest possible numbers. So if we drew the occupied spots:

.......................x...x.xx..x = 34 + 31 + 30 + 28 + 24

This is an example with n = 31, a = 5, the x mark occupied number slots and the dots (.) mark unoccupied slots. Note there are gaps. There is at least one ‘x’ that has one ‘.’ to its right. We can move that ‘x’ to that spot and the sum would increment by 1.

.......................x...x.x.x.x = 34 + 32 + 30 + 28 + 24

The only case in which this is not possible is if all of the maximum slots are in use:

.............................xxxxx = 34 + 33 + 32 + 31 + 30

But this case will not happen, because h > l.

Code

Find n, then search for a valid a value. For each a value, find the minimum possible score l and the maximum h. If l <= x <= h, then a is a valid number of turns Alice won. To find l and h we can use the summation formula or we can just accumulate them as we iterate from a = 0 to a = n

long findMinimumValue(long x, long y) {
  long t = x + y;
  long n = 0;
  while (n * (n + 1) / 2 < t) {
    n++;
  }
  if (n * (n + 1) / 2 > t) {
    return -1;
  }
  long lo = 0, hi = 0;
  for (int a = 0; a <= n; a++) {
    if (lo <= x && x <= hi) {
      return a;
    }
    lo += a + 1;
    hi += n - a;
  }

  return -1;
}

If we simply saw the space of reachable sub-rectangles as a graph, we would have O(n^4) vertices (sub-rectangles) in the graph (We are using n = max(wh) in this case). Each sub-rectangle has O(n) top rows, bottom rows, left columns and right columns to fold. For each of these possible moves, we need to check equality in O(n^2) cells to verify if the move is valid. This kind of approach leads to O(n^7) complexity which would be too large.

In problems with horizontal and vertical moves, it is often useful (if possible) to find a way to solve the horizontal and vertical problems independently. To do this, we would need to analyze the way the allowed moves work.

Vertical then Horizontal

Imagine we had two consecutive moves: A vertical move and then a horizontal move. There are some cells in the board that must be equal for this sequence of moves to be valid:

In that image the segmented lines represent the lines used to fold the board. Take that red cell #1, because of the first vertical move (that will fold the top 3 rows) then another cell has to be equal to this cell (if the cell is white, the reflected cell must be white too, if the cell is black, then the other cell must be black.).

After the vertical move, we will need to make a horizontal move, this means that there is another cell that must be equal:

Interestingly, this new cell must also be equal to a cell in the top, because the first vertical move must be valid:

Note that this means it is also possible to do the horizontal move (fold columns) BEFORE the vertical move and it will still be valid. This is because the horizontal symmetry is reflected by the vertical symmetry. Let’s try this with more cells:

The condition still holds. This means that whenever a sequence of moves consisting of a vertical move followed by a horizontal move is possible, then the sequence of moves consisting of that horizontal move followed by a vertical one will also be possible.

Independence

Imagine a sequence of moves, any sequence of moves. Some moves will be vertical, some horizontal. We represent the sequence like: VHVVHHHVHVH. Because of the finding in last section, it is always possible to, whenever you see a V followed by an H, swap their positions: VHVVHHHVHVH -> HVVVHHHVHVH -> … -> HHHHHHVVVVV. We can do all horizontal moves before all the vertical moves. The other sequence of moves VVVVVHHHHHH is also equivalent. Both HHHHHHVVVVV and VVVVVHHHHHH must be possible. Which means that HHHHHH and VVVVV must be possible. This allows us to solve horizontal and vertical moves independently. If a sequence of horizontal moves is possible in the original board and another sequence of vertical moves is also possible, then the combination of both sequences is possible too. If it is possible to remove a set of columns from the original board and it is also possible to remove a set of rows, then a combination in which those rows and columns are removed is also possible.

We can represent a sub-rectangle by the number of left and right columns and top and bottom rows that have been removed. Counting the number of reachable subrectangles is the same as counting the combinations of removed columns / rows. Because horizontal and vertical moves are independent, we can calculate the number of valid combinations of removed top/bottom rows and then the number of valid combinations of removed left/right columns and multiply them for the result.

Finding the valid combinations of rows and columns are very similar problems, in fact, we can solve them both using the same logic: Which combinations of removed top/bottom rows are valid? Then to find the left/right equivalent, just rotate the paper 90 degrees before calling the same solution. We have the beginning of our code:

vector < string > rotate(vector < string > paper) {
  int h = paper.size(), w = paper[0].size();
  vector < string > res(w, string(h, '?'));
  for (int i = 0; i < w; i++) {
    for (int j = 0; j < h; j++) {
      res[i][j] = paper[j][i];
    }
  }
  return res;
}

int howMany(vector < string > paper) {
  int resV = howManyVertical(paper);
  int resH = howManyVertical(rotate(paper)); //rotates the paper 90 degrees
  return resV * resH;
}

The vertical problem

So it is all up to solving the vertical problem: Count the valid combinations of top/bottom rows to remove.

We can use the initial graph idea. This time ignoring horizontal moves. There are O(n^2) vertices in the graph (depending on the number of removed rows at the bottom and top). Then we can try each possible move. There are O(n) candidate moves. Checking if a move is allowed is still O(n^2) , this takes us to O(n^5) complexity. This solution would be good enough for n <= 50. We can do better, however.

Top and bottom are independent too

You’ve seen how much it is possible to optimize by noticing that vertical and horizontal problems are independent. If top and bottom problems were also independent, it would help us a lot. So imagine if you could find which numbers of rows were valid to remove from the top and also from the bottom and just combine the two results. We would need O(n^2) time to combine the results.

There is an issue when showing that top and bottom are independent. Moves might overlap. Imagine if the last vertical move consisted of removing the x top rows, this would need other x rows to exist. So if the bottom moves removed some of the required 2x rows, it wouldn’t be valid. Would it? Imagine a case in which there is this overlap: We find that it is possible to remove the top 4 rows and the bottom 4 rows in one step each:

Both moves are reported to be valid when ignoring the other rows. However, apparently it should not be possible to do one after the other (a required row will be missing). Let’s try however an analysis like the one we used before:

Although this doesn’t seem interesting at first, it shows that the top row must be equal to the second-top row and also the bottom row must be equal to the second-bottom row. If they are equal, we can remove the top and bottom row. Doing this would remove the overlap between the two moves.

Generalizing this to something that we can be sure would work in all overlapping cases requires us to retake the idea that the folding symmetry lines are also mirrored. When there is an overlap, we find a folding line inside the folding area of another folding line and thus we can mirror it:

The new lines will allow moves that are shorter and contain no overlaps.

This independence helps us solve the problem with less complexity. We just need to find out which top rows can be removed and another solution for the bottom rows. We can use the same logic for top and bottom, just flip the board vertically when calculating the latter:

vector < string > flipVertical(vector < string > paper) {
  int i = 0, j = paper.size() - 1;
  while (i < j) {
    swap(paper[i], paper[j]);
    i++;
    j--;
  }
  return paper;
}

int howManyVertical(vector < string > paper) {
  //eg: topRows[i] is true if we can remove i top rows
  vector < bool > topRows = solveTop(paper); // O(n^4)
  vector < bool > bottomRows = solveTop(flipVertical(paper)); // O(n^4)
  int h = paper.size();
  int res = 0;
  for (int a = 0; a < h; a++) {
    for (int b = 0; a + b < h; b++) { //O(n^2)
      if (topRows[a] && bottomRows[b]) {
        res++;
      }
    }
  }
  return res;
}

Solving the top case

Finally we just need to solve this subproblem. We need to find how many of the top rows is it possible to remove.

We can reuse the initial idea. For each number of removable top rows, simulate the next step. There are O(n) candidates for the next step. Each needs an O(n^2) verification. This takes us to O(n^4) time:

vector < bool > solveTop(vector < string > paper) {
  int h = paper.size(), w = paper[0].size();
  vector < bool > canRemove(h);
  canRemove[0] = true;
  for (int y = 0; y < h; y++) { //O(h)
    if (canRemove[y]) {
      // we removed y top rows, find the remaining moves
      for (int m = 1; m + m <= h - y; m++) { //O(h^2)
        bool valid = true; // check if the move is possible

        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) { //O(h^3)
          for (int j = 0; j < w; j++) { //O(h^3 * w)
            valid &= (paper[y + i][j] == paper[y + m + m - i - 1][j]);
          }
        }
        if (valid) {
          canRemove[y + m] = true;
        }
      }
    }
  }

  return canRemove;
}

Cubic complexity

What if we want better? Note that most of the problem is O(n^2), the only complex part is the solveTop function which is currently O(n^4). Can it be optimized to O(n^3)? (This solves the division 1 version of the problem). The trick is to note that the O(n^2) check to see if a move is valid can be done in O(n) time: It spends a whole O(n) factor comparing each pair of rows. If, instead we precalculate the equality results of O(n^2) pairs of rows (precalculating this would need O(n^3) time, because for each O(n^2) pair, it needs an O(n) check). Then we can use the precalculated data to check if a move is valid in O(n) time.

vector < bool > solveTop(vector < string > paper) {
  int h = paper.size(), w = paper[0].size();
  vector < bool > canRemove(h);
  canRemove[0] = true;

  // precalculate equality for each pair of rows:
  vector < vector < bool >> equalRows(h, vector < bool > (h));

  for (int i = 0; i < h; i++) { //O(h)
    for (int j = 0; j < h; j++) { //O(h*h)
      equalRows[i][j] = true;
      for (int k = 0; k < w; k++) { //O(h*h*w)
        equalRows[i][j] = (equalRows[i][j] && (paper[i][k] == paper[j][k]));
      }
    }
  }

  for (int y = 0; y < h; y++) { //O(h)
    if (canRemove[y]) {
      // we removed y top rows, find the remaining moves
      for (int m = 1; m + m <= h - y; m++) { //O(h^2)
        bool valid = true; // check if the move is possible

        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) { //O(h^3)
          valid &= equalRows[y + i][y + m + m - i - 1];
        }
        if (valid) {
          canRemove[y + m] = true;
        }
      }
    }
  }

  return canRemove;
}

O(n ^ 2) is possible. Once again, it is all up to optimize the solveTop function. With hashing, we can do the precalculation in O(n^2) time. Then we can actually precalculate the validity of a move (consisting of folding a range of rows) in O(n^2) using a recursive logic that you might find to be an interesting exercise.

AliceGame

Problem Details

Used as: Division One - Level One:

Value250

Like in the division 2 version, it is possible to determine n, the total number of turns. Just do t = x + y and this time look for an n such that sum_(i=1)^(i<=n)(2i - 1) = t. We can find a formula for this special summation:

sum_(i=1)^(i<=n)(2i - 1) = = sum_(i=1)^(i<=n)(2i) + sum_(i=1)^(i<=n)(-1) = 2sum_(i=1)^(i<=n)(i) - sum_(i=1)^(i<=n)(1) = 2( (n(n+1)) / 2) - n = (n(n+1) - n = n^2

So in fact, n = sqrt(t).

The rest is still similar to division 2, we can do a search for the smallest valid a. Now we need to answer if given n, x and a, it is possible for Alice to score x by winning exactly a turns.

The difficulty is that now turns give 2i - 1 points instead of just i turns. Although it is possible to solve the problem with this setup, it might be simpler to convert the problem to the i version which we already solved in division 2. The first insight is that every turn that Alice wins will bring one -1 to the sum. So if Alice wins exactly a turns, then her score will be something like (2x_1 - 1) + (2x_2 - 1) + … (2x_a - 1) = (2x_1 + 2x_2 + … 2x_a) + a(-1) = 2(x_1 + x_2 + … x_a) - a = x. Where x_j is the j-th turn Alice won. Now notice that after adding a to Alice’s score x, we will have: 2(x_1 + x_2 + … x_a), x_1 + x_2 + … + x_a is the sum of the turn numbers Alice won. Therefore:

  • x - a must be even.

  • (x - a) / 2 must be a valid score in the version in which each win awards i points (the explanation for the division 2 version explains how to know if this is valid: Just find the lower and upper bounds for the sums of a turn numbers and check that (x-a)/2 is within the bounds)

And that’s it:

long findMinimumValue(long x, long y) {
  //2*i - 1
  // a) find out how many games there were, x + y points in total
  long t = x + y;
  // find n : sum(2*i) - n = t
  //         2*sum(i)  - n = t
  //         2*(n(n+1))/2-n = t
  //         n(n+1) - n = t
  //          n(n+1-1)  = t
  //              n^2   = t // ah funny

  int n = 0;
  while (n * (long) n < t) {
    n++;
  }
  if (n * (long) n > t) {
    return -1;
  }
  // now we know how many turns, which ones went to Alice?
  long bottom = 0, top = 0;
  for (int a = 0; a <= n; a++) {
    if ((x - a) % 2 == 0) {
      if (bottom <= (x - a) / 2 && (x - a) / 2 <= top) {
        return a;
      }
    }
    bottom += a; // Alice wins the "a" first games
    top += n - a; // Alice wins the "a" last games
  }

  return -1;
}

Div2 250: BacteriesColony

Problem Details

The problem is a simulation problem but there are a couple of details to consider. Take a look to the second example: {1, 5, 4, 9}.

In the first night, we have that 1 and 4 are smaller than 5, so 5 should decrease: {1,4,4,9}. This creates a mistake, because then we will look at 4 between 4 and 9 and say nothing should happen (4 is equal to 4 and both numbers to each side need to be smaller or greater).

So the changes happen in parallel, we should save the decision to decrease 5, but use the outdated bacteria counts for the rest of the simulation of that night. We can do this by creating a copy of the bacteria counts: nw = “colonies”.

{1, 5, 4, 9} = "colonies" {1, 5, 4, 9} = nw

First we decrease 5, but in the copy:

{1, 5, 4, 9} = "colonies" {1, 4, 4, 9} = nw

For i = 2, we check both 5 and 9 (in the old colonies vector). This time we have that 4 is smaller than both 5 and 9, so it needs to be increased in the new copy:

{1, 5, 4, 9} = "colonies" {1, 4, 5, 9} = nw

Keeping the copy is also useful to know when to stop the simulation. If after a night, nw = “colonies” then we can tell there were no changes. Else we continue the simulation, a new night means creating a new copy, but first we must update colonies to be equal to the copy we made in the previous night.

vector < int > performTheExperiment(vector < int > colonies) {
  vector < int > nw;
  while (true) {
    nw = colonies;
    // simulate a night:
    for (int i = 1; i + 1 < colonies.size(); i++) {
      if (colonies[i - 1] > colonies[i] && colonies[i + 1] > colonies[i]) {
        nw[i]++;
      }
      if (colonies[i - 1] < colonies[i] && colonies[i + 1] < colonies[i]) {
        nw[i]--;
      }
    }
    if (colonies == nw) {
      // break when equal
      break;
    }
    colonies = nw;
  }
  return colonies;
}

Div2 Medium: ConnectingCars

Problem Details

The key idea in this problem is to understand that there always exists an optimal-cost solution in which one of the cars doesn’t move at all. Maybe some other solutions in which all cars move have optimal cost. But there will always be one in which a car stays in place But how can we find this out and how can it be proven?

Consider the cost function to move a single car to a point. If the final point is x and the original point is p then the cost will be | p - x |. If we draw this absolute value function we will find a triangle and the point x = p will be the minimum point.

The total cost function will be the addition of many absolute value functions. In each of these functions, the minimum point will be coincide with the starting point of a car. When we add these functions together, these points will be the ones in which there might be changes in slope, and thus candidates to contain the minimum cost.

Once we assume that one of the cars shouldn’t move, we can try each car i and find the cost in case car i does not move. Imagine the cars are sorted by their starting point. So all cars j < i will start to the left of car i and we should compress them. The same happens with cars j > i, this time compressing them to the right:

Let’ do the left-compression, we first try car j = i-1, then j = i-2 and so and so. Every time placing car j to the right-most possible position (without exceeding the position of the previous car we placed).

  • Initially, we set L = p_i, the left limit currently is the start of car i.

  • For each j, we should move the car to position L - “lengths”[j]. We can calculate this cost. L now decreases by “lengths”[j] and we can go on.

For the right side, we can do a similar process, this time from j = i+1, i+2, …. And keeping a R , right variable.

long minimizeCost(vector < int > positions, vector < int > lengths) {
  // we can use bubble sort, it's not optimal, but it is a simple way
  // to sort both vectors at once. Each language has specific better ways to do this.
  int n = positions.size();
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
      if (positions[j] < positions[i]) {
        swap(positions[j], positions[i]);
        swap(lengths[j], lengths[i]);
      }
    }
  }

  long res = 2 LL << 61;
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    // car #i doesn't move.
    long cost = 0;
    int left = positions[i] + lengths[i];
    for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
      cost += abs(left - positions[j]);
      left += lengths[j];
    }

    int right = positions[i];
    for (int j = i - 1; j >= 0; j--) {
      cost += abs(right - (positions[j] + lengths[j]));
      right -= lengths[j];
    }
    res = std::min(res, cost);
  }
  return res;
}

Div2 Hard: JanuszInTheCasino

Problem Details

There are two parts in this problem. The first is to find the optimal strategy for each round - What should the players do to maximize the probability that at least one of them will remain.

This part is solved by trying to distribute the n players’ coins among the m spaces evenly. Each space will have at least |__n / m__| (floor function, meaning that this is the division rounding down). There will be exactly n % m (remainder after dividing) spaces that will have |~n / m~| players (ceil function, rounding up).

The intuition to reach this idea is based on how, the larger n after step i, then the probability can only increase. If we distribute the players evenly, this keeps the worst reduction in players as small as possible. In the worst case, one of the spaces containing |~n / m~| players will be picked, so n will drop by |~n / m~| . For example if we define f(n,k) as the minimum probability if we play k rounds and initially have n players:

f(n,k) >= f( n - |~n/m~| , k-1)

This is a bound that is difficult to beat through another strategy. Because in any other strategy, the worst possible drop in the number of players is worse.

Dynamic programming

Assuming that strategy is correct, how can we calculate the probability?

Depending on which space is picked, the number of players drops by |__n/m__| or |n/m|. There are n%m spaces with |n/m| players. This means that the probability to pick them is: p = (n%m) / m.

f(n,k) = p f(n - |~n/m~|, k-1) + (1 - p) f(n - |__n/m__|, k-1)

(If you use f(n - |__n/m__|, k-1) >= f(n - |n/m|, k-1) , this proves the inequality above).

We have a base case when k = 0. There are no rounds to play. This means that the players win if and only if n > 0.

Evaluating this function appears to be difficult. Each round apparently multiplies the number of branches by 2. The initial value for n is 10^12, so straightforward memoization isn’t possible either.

The key lies in understanding the function definition above and note that the distinct values of n that are passed to the function won’t be very varied. How can we tell?

Imagine we initially have a single n, let’s say 52. Our m is 7. In the first step we move to n - |__n/m__| and n + |n/m|. In this case: 52 - 7 = 45 and 52 - 8 = 44.

Now try with 44 and 45. The new possible values after subtracting, from each candidate of n, the floor and ceiling of n/m is: 37, 38, 39. What we need to understand is the result of the possible candidates is always an interval.

Let’s go on. To find the new candidates after 37, 38, 39, note that we can find the resulting interval’s ends by reducing the most from 37 and adding the least to 39: 31, 32, 33, 34 . In each round, the interval size increases by at most 2. We can use this to learn there are O(N) variations of n in f(n,k). Then we can use simply user memoization using your language’s associative array. The following c++ solution uses std::map.

// this map stores the results for each (n,k)
map < pair < int, long > , double > mem;
double findProbability(long n, int m, int k) {
  // result know?
  bool seen = mem.count({
    n,
    k
  });
  // A reference to the result
  double & res = mem[{
    n,
    k
  }];
  if (!seen) {
    // evaluate this state
    if (k == 0) { // base case
      if (n >= 1) {
        res = 1.0;
      } else {
        res = 0.0;
      }
    } else {
      double p = (n % m) / (double) m;
      res = p * findProbability(n - (n / m + 1), m, k - 1) +
        (1 - p) * findProbability(n - (n / m), m, k - 1);
    }
  }
  return res;
}

Div1 Easy: JanuszTheBusinessman

Problem Details

What is really helpful in this problem is to think of the departure times. Specifically, the earliest possible departure time. If this is a lone customer, we HAVE to bribe them. If they were multiple customers departing at the same day, we have to bribe one of them, the rest will become satisfied by contact. But if there were multiple customers at that departure day, including some whose departure time would come much later, then we can pay one of those other customers and the customers who have the current departure will be happy too.

Then the problem becomes, given the earliest departure time d, which customer whose stay time includes d be bribed? We only need to bribe one of them, and whichever is bribed, the customers whose departure time is d will be happy. So we need to choose thinking on future choices we might need to pay. If we pick the customer with the latest departure time, we will cover a greater range of days and we will likely cover for more customers.

Now imagine you pay for this one customer we picked. Then you can tell that there are plenty of happy customers. You can just remove / ignore them. And now we have a new earliest departure time d, we can repeat the logic.

The last issue is how to implement this logic. Turns out even straightforward simulation can be done in O(n^2) time as follows:

int makeGuestsReturn(vector < int > arrivals, vector < int > departures) {
  set < int > end(departures.begin(), departures.end());
  int n = arrivals.size();
  int open = -1;
  int cost = 0;
  vector < bool > happy(n, false);
  // For each end time:
  for (int d: end) {
    if (open == -1) {
      // find if there's any customer whose stay ends now and is not
      // already happy
      bool must = false;
      for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (departures[i] == d && !happy[i]) {
          must = true;
        }
      }
      if (must) {
        // find the best customer to bribe
        cost++;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
          // custoemr with latest departure whose stay contains d
          if (arrivals[i] <= d && d <= departures[i]) {
            if ((open == -1) || (departures[open] < departures[i])) {
              open = i;
            }
          }
        }
        // tag all customers that share stay with "open" happy.
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
          if ((arrivals[i] <= arrivals[open] && arrivals[open] <= departures[i]) ||
            (arrivals[open] <= arrivals[i] && arrivals[i] <= departures[open])
          ) {
            happy[i] = true;
          }
        }
      }
    }
    // If the "open" customer's stay ends, close it
    if (departures[open] == d) {
      open = -1;
    }
  }
  return cost;
}

Div1 Medium: ArmyTeleportation

Problem Details

The first step is to understand what can happen to a single point after applying many reflections (teleportations). A method is to grab paper and try consecutive reflections for a while. I have created an interactive applet that shows what happens to a point after one and two reflections:

(Link to interactive applet)

The other method is through math. Let’S use both at once. When generating all the points that can be reached by N reflections starting from a point X, it starts to look like the set of reachable points makes a grid. This is not entirely true and it can be confusing until you notice that there is a difference between the grid generated by an even number of reflections and an odd number of reflections.

Imagine your current point is X = (x,y), we use the reflection through point A = (A_x, A_y) and then through point B = (B_x, B_y). First what is a reflection through A? It helps to think in terms of vectors. There is a vector A - X, this vector is added twice to vector X: X + 2(A - X) = 2A - X. Reflecting (2A - X) through B will be: 2B - (2A - X) = X - 2A + 2B = X - 2(A - B). This means that in two moves, we can translate X = (x,y) to (x - 2(A_x - B_x), y - 2(A_y - B_y)). Repeating this process in all directions will reveal a nice truth: By using an even number of moves, we can take X to X + 2(A- B), X - 2(A-B), X + 2(B - C), X - 2(B - C), X + 2(A - C), X - 2(C - A). These are the points that make the grid in the graphic version. So let dx_1 = 2(A_x - C_x), dy_1 = 2(A_y - C_y), dx_2 = 2(B_x - C_x), dy_2 = 2(B_y - C_y). In two moves we can move from (x,y) to (x +- dx_1, y +- dy_1) and (x +- dx_2, y +- dy_2). If we keep using these 2 moves we can take X to any point in the grid: (x + i dx_1 + j dx_2, y + i dy_1 + j dx_2)

When there are multiple points, we face the same properties, just keep in mind ALL points are translated by exactly the same vectors. This also helps us find which point will become which after the translation(s), the bottom-left point will remain the bottom-left point no matter what factors of dx_1, dy_1, dx_2, dy_2 we add to each of the points. (We should add the exact same factors to each of the points). From this we have an approach to check if it is possible to reach a configuration of points after an even number of teleportations: First sort both the original points and the destination points in row-major order. Then check if the offset between each original point and its respective destination is always constant. Finally, check if the offset can be achieved by the formula above.

What about odd number of teleportations? Just note that the odd grid works in the same way, with 2(A - C) and 2(B - C) offsets as the even grid, the difference is that it is reflected (just reflecting the original points once by either A , B or C and then checking if the new set of points can be turned into the intended destination set of points using an even number of moves (same approach as before).

All that remains is how to find if an offset (dx,dy) is valid or not. We need to find a solution to : dx = i dx_1 + j dx_2, dy = i dy_1 + j_dy_2. It is a system of two linear equations with two variables, but we need i and j to be integer and we need be careful about precision. We only need to solve this question at most twice, so there is plenty of time allocated and even brute force for i can work, like in the following solution:

long dx1, dy1, dx2, dy2;

bool can_offset_onevar(long dx, long dy) {
  long d = ((dx1 != 0) ? (dx / dx1) : (dy / dy1));
  return (dx == dx1 * d && dy == dy1 * d);
}

bool can_offset(long dx, long dy) {
  int MAX = 5000000;
  for (int i = -MAX; i <= MAX; i++) {
    if (can_offset_onevar(dx - dx2 * i, dy - dy2 * i)) {
      return true;
    }
  }
  return false;
}

string ifPossible(vector < int > x1, vector < int > y1, vector < int > x2, vector < int > y2, vector < int > xt, vector < int > yt) {
  int N = x1.size();

  vector < pair < int, int > > from;
  for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    from.push_back({
      x1[i],
      y1[i]
    });
  }
  sort(from.begin(), from.end());

  vector < pair < int, int > > to;
  for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    to.push_back({
      x2[i],
      y2[i]
    });
  }
  sort(to.begin(), to.end());

  dx1 = (xt[0] - xt[2]) * 2;
  dy1 = (yt[0] - yt[2]) * 2;
  dx2 = (xt[1] - xt[2]) * 2;
  dy2 = (yt[1] - yt[2]) * 2;

  for (int j = 0; j < 2; j++) {
    if (j == 1) {
      // move to the odd space:
      for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        from[i].first = 2 * xt[0] - from[i].first;
        from[i].second = 2 * yt[0] - from[i].second;
      }
      sort(from.begin(), from.end());
    }

    long dx = to[0].first - from[0].first;
    long dy = to[0].second - from[0].second;

    bool failed = false;
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
      if (to[i].first - from[i].first != dx) {
        failed = true;
      }
      if (to[i].second - from[i].second != dy) {
        failed = true;
      }
    }

    if (!failed && can_offset(dx, dy)) {
      return "possible";
    }

  }

  return "impossible";
}

Div1 Hard: AquaparkPuzzle

Problem Details

Implementation detail: subsets of a sufficiently small set can be encoded as bitsets - integers and some operations on them as common bitwise operations on integers. Thanks to processor architecture, these bitwise operations take constant (a very small constant) time.

We’ll denote binomial coefficients - the number of ways to choose k elements out of n - by C(n,k).

First thoughts

A tip on using intuition: when you don’t know how to solve it, try to solve a simpler version of it and try to generalise the approach you took.

In this case, we’re asked to count the number of ways in which we can choose visits (let’s call them plans) in such a way that each attraction is visited at least twice. What if we only wanted to visit each attraction at least once?

That problem has a simple solution using the inclusion-exclusion principle (you may read more about it here), which claims that we can compute the answer by considering all subsets S of attractions, for each of them counting the number of plans for K days that don’t visit any attractions in S at all (regardless of how many times the attractions outside of that set are visited), adding up these numbers for sets S of even size and subtracting them for S of odd size.

Think about what we’re counting: for empty S, we add the total number of plans for K days. But that means we included plans that don’t visit some attraction at all, so we need to subtract their numbers for all possible non-visited attractions (S of size 1). But that means we subtracted twice plans that don’t visit two attractions, so we need to add those back - etc.

The number of allowed plans for K days that don’t visit any attraction from a given set is just the number of such plans for one day to the power of K, which can be counted easily.

We can see that the problem was significantly simplified thanks to the I-E principle, so what we want to modify is the use of this principle. When the desired number of visits was at least one, we used it to count plans where subsets of attractions were visited “NOT at least once”. In the original problem, the desired number of visits of each attraction is two, so we can use the I-E principle in the same way, except that we need to count plans where some attractions are visited “NOT at least twice” - at most once.

Visiting each attraction at most once

Suppose that we chose a set S of size M; we want to count the number of plans for K days that visit each attraction in S at most once. Since we don’t care about attractions outside of S, we can assign numbers from 0 to M-1 to attractions in S and view this subproblem as counting the number of plans that visit each of these M attractions at most once.

Let’s look at all plans for one day (for all N attractions, not just these M) that don’t exceed the budget. After the above renumbering and ignoring attractions outside of S, each of them describes visiting some set s \subset {0…M-1}. Of course, some plans may turn out to visit the same set and some sets may not be visited at all. Let’s say that there are P_1[s] plans visiting s in one day and in general, we’ll denote the number of plans for k days that visit each attraction in a set s exactly once by P_k[s]. This part is straightforward looping over plans for one day and attractions, so it takes O(N 2^N) time.

The number we’re looking for is the sum of P_K[s] over all s, so we want to know the array P_K[].

We can use DP to obtain P_{k+l}[] from P_k[] and P_l[]. If the set of attractions visited exactly once during the first k days is s_1 and the set visited during the next l days is s_2, then:

  • s_1 and s_2 must be disjoint - otherwise, we’d violate the condition of each attraction being visited at most once

  • the set of attractions visited during all k+l days is s_1\cup s_2 (disjoint union)

  • the number of ways to make it happen is P_k[s_1] P_l[s_2]

Therefore, we only need to iterate over all disjoint pairs (s_1,s_2) and always add P_k[s_1] P_l[s_2] to P_{k+l}[s_1\cup s_2]. Two relevant questions arise: how do we find disjoint pairs efficiently and how much time does this take?

As for finding disjoint pairs, we only need to precompute the list of s_2 for each M and s_1 - just try all s_2, check if its union with s_1 is empty (bitwise AND is zero) and store the disjoint ones in an array or something.

To determine how much time computing P_{k+l}[] takes, we need to know the number of disjoint pairs (s_1,s_2) over all subsets of {1…M-1}. There are C(M,x) subsets s_1 with size x; for each such subset, there are 2^{M-x} subsets disjoint with it. Therefore, the number of disjoint pairs is \sum_{x=0}^M{ C(M,x) 2^{M-x} }. When we notice that this sum is just an expanded form of 3M=(2+1)M from the binomial theorem, it becomes clear that there are exactly 3^M such disjoint pairs and the time complexity of computing P_{k+l}[] from P_k[] and P_l[] is O(3^M).

That’s too slow for a straightforward O(K 3^M) DP, so we’ll use an analogy of fast exponentiation. How to find P_k[] for k > 1? If k is odd, let’s compute P_{k-1}[]; we can use it together with P_1[] to compute P_k[]. If k is even, we’ll compute P_{k/2}[] instead and then compute P_k[] just from P_{k/2}[].

This way, k is at least halved in at most two steps of the recursion, each step only requires computing one array for a smaller k and we only need O( \log K ) steps to obtain P_K[] (of course, it always terminates, since we always end up only needing P_1[], which is known). The time complexity of computing P_K[] is therefore O( 3^M \log K ).

What exactly is the time complexity of the whole algorithm? The value of M changes for different sets S, so we need to write another sum over all M. There are C(N,M) sets S of size M; for each of them, we take O(N 2^N + 3^M \log K ) time. The total complexity is big O of

\sum_{M=0}^N{ C(N,M) (N 2^N + 3^M \log K )}=N 2^N \sum_{M=0}^N C(N,M) + \log K \sum_{M=0}^N{ C(N,M) 3^M}\,.

The first of these two sums is just 2^N (the total number of sets S); for the second one, we can use the same trick of viewing it as an expanded form of 4N=(3+1)N from the binomial theorem. Precomputing the disjoint pairs (s_1,s_2) takes O(N 4^N) time, so the total complexity is O( 4^N ( N + \log K ) ).

int countSchedules(int K, int B, vector < int > cost) {
  int N = cost.size();

  // precompute all disjoint s_2 for each M, s_1
  vector < vector < vector < int > > > disj(N + 1);
  for (int n = 0; n <= N; n++) {
    disj[n].resize(1 << n);
    for (int i = 0; i < (1 << n); i++)
      for (int j = 0; j < (1 << n); j++)
        if ((i & j) == 0) disj[n][i].push_back(j);
  }

  vector < in > subset_cost(1 << N, 0);
  for (int i = 0; i < (1 << N); i++)
    for (int j = 0; j < N; j++)
      if ((i >> j) & 1) subset_cost[i] += cost[j];

  long long ans = 0, mod = 1000000007;

  // inclusion-exclusion
  for (int s = (1 << N) - 1; s >= 0; s--) {
    // each attraction in s visited at most once

    // compress attractions in s to 0..M-1
    vector < int > attractions_in_s;
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++)
      if (((s >> i) & 1) == 0)
        attractions_in_s.push_back(i);
    int M = attractions_in_s.size();

    vector < long long > P(1 << M, 0); // P_1[]
    for (int s2 = 0; s2 < (1 << N); s2++)
      if (subset_cost[s2] <= B) {
        // s2: valid plan for 1 day, compressed to S
        int S = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < M; i++)
          if ((s2 >> attractions_in_s[i]) & 1)
            S += (1 << i);
        P[S]++;
      }

    vector < long long > p = P;
    stack < int > bits_K;
    int k = K;
    while (k > 0) {
      bits_K.push(k % 2);
      k /= 2;
    }
    bits_K.pop();

    while (bits_K.size() > 0) {

      // P_{k/2}[] to P_k[]
      vector < long long > P_new(1 << M, 0);
      for (int i = 0; i < (1 << M); i++)
        for (auto it = begin(disj[M][i]); it != end(disj[M][i]); it++)
          P_new[i | * it] = (P_new[i | * it] + P[i] * P[ * it]) % mod;
      for (int i = 0; i < (1 << M); i++) {
        if (P_new[i] < 0) P_new[i] += mod;
        P[i] = P_new[i];
        P_new[i] = 0;
      }

      // P_k[] to P_{k+1}[]
      if (bits_K.top() == 1) {
        for (int i = 0; i < (1 << M); i++)
          for (auto it = begin(disj[M][i]); it != end(disj[M][i]); it++)
            P_new[i | * it] = (P_new[i | * it] + P[i] * p[ * it]) % mod;
        for (int i = 0; i < (1 << M); i++) {
          if (P_new[i] < 0) P_new[i] += mod;
          P[i] = P_new[i];
        }
      }

      bits_K.pop();
    }

    for (int i = 0; i < (1 << M); i++) {
      if (M % 2 == 0) ans = (ans + P[i]) % mod;
      else ans = (ans - P[i]) % mod;
    }
  }

  if (ans < 0) ans += mod;
  return ans;
}

Div2 250: TheConsecutiveIntegersDivTwo

Problem Details

k is either 1 or 2, which are simple cases. The division 1 version of the problem solves the general case, but since this is the easiest problem slot we should try as simple a solution as possible.

k = 1

In this case, we can return 0. Because any single number in the input can make a sequence of consecutive numbers of length 1.

k = 2

We don’t initially know which pairs of original from the input to use as a base, but we can try all O(n^2) such pairs. For each pair of two distinct integers x and y calculate the minimum cost to transform them into two new x’ and y’ such that the two are consecutive. If the two are consecutive, this means that y’ = x’+1 (We’ll care about trying orders when picking the pairs). So we need to find the best x’ value such that the cost of turning x into x’ and y into x’+1 is the least possible.

If x < y the best x’ is x. The cost would be just the cost to turn y into x’ + 1. Note that if we try any other value of x’, the cost doesn’t improve. With x’ = x+1 , we add 1 to the cost of fixing x and the cost of fixing y might decrease by 1 or increase by 1, neither case improves the total cost. The same happens with x’ = x-1.
